title: Stunning cliff views along the Causeway Cliff Walk path
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Hamilton's Seat is a stunning spot along the Causeway Cliff Walk, offering some of the best views on the trail. Perched high on the cliffs, this seat was built in honour of the 18th-century landowner, Sir James Hamilton, who helped establish the nearby estate and surrounding land. It’s a perfect resting point along the walk, giving you a chance to catch your breath while taking in sweeping views of the coastline, the Giant’s Causeway, and out to the Atlantic."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The spot itself is a great place to sit and soak up the beauty of the landscape, the crashing waves below, the rugged cliffs, and the expansive views of the coastline stretching out before you. It’s one of those peaceful, almost surreal moments on the Causeway Cliff Walk that makes you feel completely connected to the natural beauty of the North of Ireland."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: The seat is about halfway along the cliff path, so it’s a great place to pause before heading towards the famous Giant's Causeway or continuing along the scenic route."}]}]}

title: Historic lookout tower with stunning coastal views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Torr Head is one of the North of Ireland’s hidden gems. Located along a quiet stretch of the rugged coastline, it offers stunning views of the Irish Sea and, on clear days, the distant shores of Scotland. The drive out is along narrow, winding roads, but the scenery as you approach makes it all worth it."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"At the top of Torr Head, you'll find a historic lookout tower, a reminder of the area’s past. This old watchtower once served as a lookout during times of war, and today it offers some of the best panoramic views on the coast. From here, you can look out over the cliffs as the sea crashes below and enjoy a peaceful, almost otherworldly atmosphere. If you're after a quiet spot to soak in Northern Ireland's natural beauty (and a bit of history), Torr Head is the place to be.  Due to its location it is the closest part of the Island of ireland to Scotland, a very visible 13miles away across the water, Almost within touching distance!"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: The road leading up to Torr Head can be tricky, so take your time and enjoy the views along the way."}]}]}

title: Secluded Cove away from the crowds
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Murlough Bay is one of County Antrim’s most stunning and peaceful coastal spots, tucked away between Fair Head and Torr Head along the Antrim Coast Road. It offers sweeping views across the sea to Rathlin Island and the Mull of Kintyre in Scotland, where dramatic cliffs, quiet beaches, and green hills blend together in perfect harmony. The road down to the bay is steep and winding, but the views are unforgettable, wild, unspoiled, and timeless."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"This secluded bay is rich in history and legend. It was said to be a favourite place of Saint Columba and is also the burial site of Irish Republican figure Roger Casement, whose grave overlooks the sea he loved. Today, Murlough Bay is a peaceful retreat for walkers, photographers, and anyone wanting to experience the rugged beauty of the Antrim Coast without the crowds. It is an ideal stop for those exploring the Causeway Coastal Route, offering a glimpse of Northern Ireland at its most serene and untouched."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Step drive down to the cove so be careful on winter days"}]}]}

title: A heaven for waterfall lovers
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Glenariff Forest Park, known as the “Queen of the Glens,” is one of Ireland’s most beautiful natural attractions and most beautiful set of waterfalls. Located in County Antrim, the park features dramatic waterfalls, lush woodland trails, and panoramic views over the glen and out to the sea. Visitors can explore a network of well-marked paths, including the famous Waterfall Walkway, a scenic route that winds through deep gorges and alongside cascading falls."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perfect for walkers, photographers, and nature lovers, Glenariff offers a peaceful escape into the heart of the Antrim Glens. The park also has a visitor centre, café, and picnic areas, making it ideal for a half-day or full-day trip. Whether you are looking for a gentle stroll or a more challenging hike, Glenariff Forest Park combines natural beauty, wildlife, and tranquility in one unforgettable setting."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: A lot of steps down to the waterfall so may not be suitable for all mobilities"}]}]}

title: 350 year old rope bridge
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge is one of North of Ireland’s most thrilling coastal attractions. Spanning a narrow chasm above the Atlantic Ocean, the rope bridge connects the mainland near Ballintoy to the small Carrick Island. The walk out to the bridge takes you along rugged cliffs and dramatic coastal paths, with crashing waves below and panoramic views of the Antrim coastline. On clear days, the surrounding sea stretches out endlessly, making the journey to the bridge as breathtaking as the crossing itself."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The rope bridge was originally built by salmon fishermen over 350 years ago and has retained its historic charm while becoming a must-see visitor attraction. Crossing it is an exhilarating experience, the wooden planks sway gently underfoot as you peer down at the rocks and foaming water beneath. Once on Carrick Island, you can enjoy sweeping views of the Atlantic and watch seabirds nesting along the cliffs. For those who love a mix of natural beauty, history, and a touch of adventure, Carrick-a-Rede offers an unforgettable experience."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The bridge is exposed to the elements, so it may be closed during high winds. Timed tickets are recommended during peak season."}]}]}

title: Hike to Ireland's Tallest Rock Tower
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get ready for one of the most dramatic views in Leitrim at Eagles Rock, the tallest free-standing rock tower in Ireland. From the small car park, you'll embark on a challenging 3km round-trip hike that takes about 1.5 to 2 hours. The path across the open farmland can be steep and rugged, so you'll want proper hiking boots, but the breathtaking sight of the rock and the sweeping Glenade Valley is a magnificent reward for your efforts. It's a peaceful spot, perfect for escaping the crowds and soaking in the wild Irish landscape."}]}]}

title: Donegal's Dramatic Coastal Hike
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set along one of Donegal’s wildest stretches of coastline, the walk to "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Sturrall Headland"},{"type":"text","text":" is a truly dramatic and unforgettable adventure. This remote headland feels raw and untamed, with sheer drops, sweeping ocean views, and a striking knife-edge ridge that immediately sets it apart from more well-known coastal routes."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"This is not a marked or casual walk. Starting from the car park at "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Port"},{"type":"text","text":", the hike crosses rugged, uneven terrain and requires careful navigation, a steady footing, and a good head for heights. The route is challenging throughout, but every climb is rewarded with breathtaking views of the wild Atlantic crashing far below and the dramatic shape of the headland unfolding ahead."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Sturrall Headland is best suited to experienced hikers and confident adventurers looking for a genuine wilderness experience. Allow at least "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"three hours"},{"type":"text","text":" to fully enjoy the walk, taking time to pause, take in the scenery, and move carefully along steeper sections. The sense of isolation and scale here is powerful, making the journey as memorable as the destination."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For the best experience, choose a clear summer’s day when visibility is good and conditions are dry, allowing the dramatic landscape to be fully appreciated. Calm weather transforms the walk into something truly special, with far-reaching coastal views and a rare feeling of standing at the edge of the land."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" This is an exposed and unmarked route with steep drops and changing conditions. Proper hiking footwear, suitable weather, and confidence in navigation are essential, and the walk should be avoided in poor visibility or high winds."}]}]}

title: The North of Irelands most famous spot
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Giant’s Causeway is one of the North of Ireland’s most iconic landmarks and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, located on the north coast of County Antrim. It is made up of around 40,000 interlocking basalt columns formed by volcanic activity millions of years ago, creating a natural wonder that looks almost otherworldly. The unique hexagonal stones stretch out into the sea, and on a clear day, the coastline and crashing waves make for truly breathtaking scenery."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Legend tells that the causeway was built by the giant Finn McCool, who wanted to cross the sea to Scotland to fight his rival, Benandonner. Whether you come for the myth or the geology, the site has an unforgettable atmosphere. Visitors can explore the walking trails along the cliffs, visit the award-winning visitor centre, and enjoy panoramic views of the Atlantic Ocean. The Giant’s Causeway is a must-see destination for anyone exploring the Antrim Coast."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: This is the North of Ireland’s most famous spot so it more than likely will be busy "}]}]}

title: Historic Lighthouse & Coastal Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perched at the tip of the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Fanad Peninsula"},{"type":"text","text":", "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Fanad Head Lighthouse"},{"type":"text","text":" is one of Donegal’s most iconic coastal landmarks. Standing sentinel over the wild Atlantic, this historic lighthouse has guided sailors safely along the rugged coastline since 1817 and remains a striking symbol of maritime heritage."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visitors can explore the lighthouse and its surrounding grounds, which offer "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"stunning panoramic views"},{"type":"text","text":" across Lough Swilly and out to the open Atlantic. The combination of the lighthouse’s bright white tower, the rocky coastline, and the crashing waves creates a scene that’s both dramatic and photogenic."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For those wanting a more immersive experience, the lighthouse offers "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"guided tours and accommodations"},{"type":"text","text":", allowing guests to stay overnight and witness the raw beauty of the Donegal coast up close. Walking along the cliffs or the nearby coastal trails gives a sense of isolation and scale, highlighting the rugged and untamed nature of this part of Ireland."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The lighthouse is exposed to strong winds, particularly in winter, so warm layers and sturdy footwear are recommended. Guided tours and accommodations may require booking in advance, especially during peak season."}]}]}

title: The hidden village of Galboly
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The hidden village of Galboly is one of County Antrim’s best-kept secrets, tucked high on the slopes of the hills above Glenarm and the Antrim Coast Road. This deserted clachan, or small farming settlement, offers a fascinating glimpse into rural life from centuries past. The stone cottages, now roofless and covered in grass, sit quietly among the rolling fields with views that stretch out to the sea. Walking through Galboly feels like stepping back in time, surrounded by the echoes of a once-thriving mountain community."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The village was abandoned in the early 20th century, and nature has slowly reclaimed it, adding to its quiet, haunting beauty. It is reached by a steep, scenic path that rewards visitors with both history and breathtaking views. Galboly remains largely untouched and rarely crowded, making it a perfect stop for those seeking a more hidden, authentic side of the Antrim Glens."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Not all buildings are original and some has been added for film set purposes but I still think it adds to the character and charm of the little hamlet"}]}]}

title: Bakery and Specialty Coffee
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Ursa Minor Bakehouse is a beloved artisan bakery and café nestled in the heart of Ballycastle, County Antrim. Located at 45 Ann Street, BT54 6AA, this establishment is renowned for its commitment to traditional baking methods, using only three core ingredients, flour, water, and salt to craft exceptional sourdough bread. Their menu evolves weekly, featuring a selection of seasonal pastries, sweet bakes, and light meals, all prepared with locally sourced ingredients. The café offers a cosy atmosphere where visitors can enjoy their treats alongside a curated selection of provisions and cookbooks."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Opening Hours:"}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"bulletList","content":[{"type":"listItem","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tuesday to Saturday: 9:00 AM – 4:00 PM"}]}]},{"type":"listItem","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Sunday & Monday: Closed"}]}]}]}]}

title: Ireland's Ancient Wildlife Sanctuary
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set in the heart of the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Inishowen Peninsula"},{"type":"text","text":", "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Wild Ireland"},{"type":"text","text":" is a unique wildlife sanctuary that brings visitors face to face with animals that once roamed ancient Ireland. Nestled in a rugged, natural landscape near Burnfoot, the park offers an immersive experience where conservation, education, and the wild beauty of Donegal come together."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Wild Ireland is home to species such as "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"brown bears, wolves, lynx, wild boar, and red deer"},{"type":"text","text":", all living in spacious, natural habitats designed to mirror their native environments. Guided tours lead visitors along forest trails and boardwalks, sharing fascinating stories about each animal and their role in Ireland’s natural and mythological past."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The experience is both engaging and educational, making it ideal for families, wildlife lovers, and anyone curious about Ireland’s ancient ecosystems. The setting itself—woodland paths, lakes, and mountain backdrops—adds to the sense of stepping into a wilder time."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For the best experience, allow plenty of time for the guided tour and dress appropriately for outdoor walking, as the sanctuary is fully open to the elements."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Visits are by guided tour only, and advance booking is recommended, especially during weekends and school holidays."}]}]}

title: Wild Atlantic Seaweed Baths
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Wild Atlantic Seaweed Baths in Doolin offer a truly elemental coastal experience, where you soak in traditional wooden casks right at the water’s edge. Set along the Clare shoreline near the Cliffs of Moher, this outdoor bathing ritual combines warm seawater, hand-harvested Atlantic seaweed, and the sights and sounds of the ocean just metres away."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Each individual cask is filled with hot water and fresh seaweed, releasing natural oils and minerals that are known to ease tired muscles and nourish the skin. Sitting in the barrel, with waves nearby and sea air all around you, the experience feels both grounding and deeply relaxing especially after a day spent walking cliffs or exploring the Burren."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You don’t need any prior experience with seaweed bathing to enjoy it. The cask baths are accessible and welcoming, suitable for individuals, couples, and small groups looking for something restorative and uniquely Irish. The simplicity of the setup adds to the charm, keeping the focus on nature rather than luxury."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Advance booking is recommended. Guests should bring swimwear, towels, and footwear suitable for uneven, wet ground. As the baths are outdoors at the water’s edge, sessions are weather-dependent."}]}]}

title: Ireland's Grand Garden Hotel
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 5-star luxury hotel"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" Set at the foot of the Wicklow Mountains near Enniskerry, Powerscourt Hotel combines refined elegance with breathtaking natural surroundings, offering luxurious rooms, award-winning dining, and direct access to the iconic Powerscourt Estate and Gardens."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Powerscourt Hotel is a five-star luxury retreat blending classic Irish sophistication with modern comfort. Nestled amid the stunning landscapes of County Wicklow, it offers guests spectacular views of the Wicklow Mountains and the expansive Powerscourt Estate, including its world-famous gardens and waterfall. The hotel’s architecture and interiors reflect timeless elegance, with stylishly appointed rooms, suites, and penthouses that balance luxury with a welcoming, relaxing atmosphere."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation features individually designed rooms with plush bedding, contemporary amenities, and marble bathrooms. Many rooms provide panoramic views of the gardens or mountains, while the suites offer additional space, refined décor, and indulgent touches ideal for romantic escapes or special celebrations."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Powerscourt Hotel is a highlight, with Michelin-quality cuisine served in elegant surroundings. The Avoca Restaurant emphasizes locally sourced ingredients, while the intimate terrace and bar areas offer relaxed settings for cocktails, afternoon tea, or casual dining. Guests can also enjoy exclusive experiences such as guided garden tours, spa treatments, and outdoor activities within the estate grounds."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its combination of luxurious accommodation, award-winning gastronomy, and iconic natural and historic surroundings, Powerscourt Hotel is perfect for couples, families, and anyone seeking a quintessentially Irish luxury escape. It offers the ideal balance of relaxation, exploration, and indulgence in one of Ireland’s most scenic locations."}]}]}

title: Luxurious Lakeside Manor Estate
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" Five star luxury manor hotel"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A magnificent eighteenth century manor set on the shores of Lough Corrib, offering refined country house luxury on 138 acres of private estate just outside Galway city. Its most unforgettable feature is the Pullman Restaurant, housed in two original Orient Express carriages, where guests enjoy an elegant dining experience that feels like a journey back in time."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Glenlo Abbey Hotel and Estate is a grand and historic property that blends timeless elegance with contemporary five star comfort. Originally built in the eighteenth century, the manor has been carefully restored and enhanced to create an atmosphere of understated luxury, surrounded by sweeping lawns, ancient woodland, and tranquil lakeshore views. Despite its peaceful setting, it is only minutes from the vibrant heart of Galway city."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes beautifully appointed rooms and suites, each designed to reflect the classic character of the house while offering modern comforts. Many rooms enjoy views over Lough Corrib, the golf course, or the estate grounds, creating a serene and indulgent retreat for guests seeking relaxation and privacy."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining is a highlight of any stay at Glenlo Abbey. The Pullman Restaurant delivers a truly unique experience, pairing fine Irish cuisine with the romance and heritage of the restored Orient Express carriages. Additional dining options include elegant restaurant spaces within the manor and relaxed lounges for drinks and afternoon treats."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its rich history, exceptional dining, expansive grounds, and refined atmosphere, Glenlo Abbey Hotel and Estate is ideal for romantic escapes, special celebrations, and luxury breaks. It offers a rare blend of heritage, exclusivity, and five star service in one of the most beautiful settings in the west of Ireland."}]}]}

title: Live Like Royalty
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" Five star luxury castle hotel"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A world famous medieval castle set on the shores of Lough Corrib, offering an unparalleled five star experience within 350 acres of breathtaking parkland in County Mayo. Renowned for its rich history, regal interiors, and exceptional range of country pursuits, Ashford Castle delivers a once in a lifetime stay steeped in Irish heritage and refined luxury."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Ashford Castle dates back to the thirteenth century and has welcomed royalty, presidents, and discerning travellers for generations. Meticulously restored, the castle blends historic grandeur with contemporary elegance, featuring soaring stone walls, antique furnishings, roaring fireplaces, and beautifully manicured gardens that create a truly majestic atmosphere."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation consists of individually designed rooms and suites, each reflecting the castle’s heritage while offering modern indulgence. Luxurious fabrics, elegant furnishings, and stunning views over the estate, gardens, or Lough Corrib ensure an unforgettable and deeply comfortable stay. Signature suites offer expansive space and refined detailing for those seeking the ultimate castle experience."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Ashford Castle is exceptional, with a selection of fine dining and relaxed options showcasing the best of Irish produce. The George V Dining Room provides an elegant setting for gourmet cuisine, while more casual restaurants and lounges offer warm hospitality, afternoon tea, and fireside drinks in opulent surroundings."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Beyond the castle walls, guests can enjoy an extraordinary range of activities including falconry, horseback riding, golf, fishing, boating, spa experiences, and woodland walks. With its extraordinary setting, impeccable service, and immersive experiences, Ashford Castle is ideal for romantic escapes, milestone celebrations, and anyone seeking the pinnacle of luxury hospitality in Ireland."}]}]}

title: Historic Irish Castle Escape
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" Four star luxury castle hotel"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A majestic medieval castle set on 180 acres of countryside in County Kildare, offering an immersive historic experience with elegant interiors, championship golf, and refined dining. Dating back to the twelfth century, Kilkea Castle combines ancient heritage with modern luxury in one of Ireland’s most atmospheric castle settings."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Kilkea Castle is one of Ireland’s oldest inhabited castles and has been carefully restored to preserve its medieval character while introducing contemporary comforts. Stone walls, vaulted ceilings, open fires, and antique details create a dramatic yet welcoming atmosphere throughout the castle and its surrounding estate."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes a range of distinctive rooms and suites located within the castle and courtyard lodges. Each space is individually styled, blending historic features with plush furnishings and modern amenities. Many rooms enjoy views of the estate, gardens, or golf course, offering a peaceful and indulgent retreat."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Kilkea Castle focuses on locally sourced Irish ingredients served in elegant surroundings. Guests can enjoy refined dining experiences within the castle as well as more relaxed meals in the bistro style spaces, all complemented by warm hospitality and carefully curated menus."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Beyond accommodation and dining, Kilkea Castle offers a wide range of activities including an eighteen hole championship golf course, spa and wellness facilities, walking trails, and nearby equestrian pursuits. With its rich history, scenic setting, and luxurious comfort, Kilkea Castle is ideal for romantic escapes, special celebrations, and memorable countryside breaks."}]}]}

title: Historic Irish Castle Escape
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star Rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What Makes It Unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" One of Ireland’s last great family-owned castle estates, offering authentic aristocratic charm, a world-renowned equestrian centre, and an untouched, old-world atmosphere set on 1,000 acres of rolling Irish countryside."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Overview:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":"Castle Leslie Estate is a distinguished 4-star castle retreat in County Monaghan, steeped in over 300 years of history. Still owned by the Leslie family, the estate retains its original character and grandeur, offering guests a rare opportunity to stay in a castle that feels both stately and warmly lived-in. Surrounded by lakes, ancient woodland, and manicured grounds, it delivers a timeless escape into Ireland’s heritage."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Accommodation:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":"The estate offers a variety of accommodations across the Castle, The Lodge, and charming Old Stable Mews. Each room is individually designed with antique furnishings, rich fabrics, and period details, while modern comforts ensure a luxurious stay. Castle rooms offer a more opulent, historic experience, while The Lodge provides contemporary country-house elegance."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Dining:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Castle Leslie Estate is refined yet relaxed, with seasonal menus celebrating local Irish produce. Elegant dining rooms, cosy bars, and traditional afternoon tea experiences add to the estate’s classic country-house charm."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Experience:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":"Renowned for its equestrian centre, one of the finest in Europe, the estate is a haven for horse lovers. Guests can also enjoy lakeside walks, fishing, cycling, spa treatments, and simply soaking in the peaceful grandeur of the surroundings. The atmosphere is authentic, romantic, and steeped in heritage rather than overly modernised luxury."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ideal For:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":"Couples seeking a romantic castle stay, equestrian enthusiasts, history lovers, and guests wanting a genuine Irish estate experience with understated luxury."}]}]}

title: Rooftop Views and Italian Bites
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For some of the best panoramic views of the city, head up to Tetto, a stylish rooftop restaurant and garden bar. It's a fantastic spot for soaking up the Belfast skyline while enjoying fresh, Italian-inspired dishes like sourdough pizza and creative cocktails. The atmosphere is modern and vibrant, especially on weekends when DJs are playing, making it perfect for a fun night out or a laid-back brunch."}]}]}

title: Fairytale Castle Stay
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury castle hotel"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A fully restored 19th-century castle offering historic grandeur, opulent interiors, and modern luxury in a romantic, woodland-framed setting near Kingscourt, County Cavan."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Cabra Castle"},{"type":"text","text":" is a four-star boutique castle hotel set amid 1,000 acres of private woodland and gardens in northern County Cavan. Originally built in the early 1800s, the castle has been meticulously restored, blending period elegance with contemporary comforts to create a regal yet welcoming retreat. Its dramatic turrets, grand halls, and antique furnishings evoke a true castle experience, making it a destination in its own right."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes individually styled rooms and suites with luxurious beds, elegant décor, and modern en-suite bathrooms. Many rooms offer views of the surrounding forests and gardens, while the Castle Suite provides an extra touch of indulgence with additional space and refined furnishings, perfect for special occasions or romantic getaways."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Cabra Castle is an experience in itself. The restaurant serves fine cuisine with locally sourced ingredients, while afternoon tea and casual lounges provide elegant yet relaxed settings for refreshments. Guests can enjoy a full castle experience with attentive, personalised service throughout their stay."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its combination of historic charm, luxurious accommodation, and expansive natural setting, Cabra Castle is ideal for couples, families, and anyone looking to experience Ireland’s castle heritage without sacrificing modern comfort. It’s a place to unwind, explore the grounds, and immerse yourself in the romance of a true Irish castle."}]}]}

title: Coastal Paradise on the Ring of Kerry
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" Four star luxury coastal resort"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A secluded seaside retreat set on a private peninsula overlooking Kenmare Bay in County Kerry. Parknasilla offers a rare combination of coastal scenery, woodland walks, private beaches, and a world class spa, creating a peaceful escape surrounded by unspoilt natural beauty along the Wild Atlantic Way."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Parknasilla Resort and Spa has a long history as one of Ireland’s most iconic coastal retreats. Surrounded by subtropical gardens, rugged shoreline, and sweeping sea views, the resort feels wonderfully remote while still offering every modern comfort. Its setting makes it a destination in its own right, perfect for switching off and reconnecting with nature."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes a variety of rooms and suites within the main hotel as well as self catering lodges and villas set throughout the estate. Many rooms enjoy panoramic views of Kenmare Bay or the gardens, while all offer comfortable furnishings and contemporary amenities designed for relaxation."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Parknasilla focuses on fresh local produce inspired by the flavours of the coast. Guests can enjoy refined dining with sea views, casual meals in relaxed settings, and cosy lounges for drinks and light bites. The emphasis is on quality, seasonality, and an unhurried dining experience."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The resort’s spa and leisure facilities are a highlight, featuring indoor and outdoor thermal areas, a swimming pool, and a range of treatments designed to soothe and restore. With coastal walks, water activities, and tranquil surroundings, Parknasilla Resort and Spa is ideal for couples, families, and anyone seeking a luxurious escape immersed in nature and serenity."}]}]}

title: Large forest estate hotel
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury country estate"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A sprawling lakeside estate combining elegant accommodation, championship golf, outdoor activities, and tranquil gardens in scenic Cavan countryside."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Farnham Estate"},{"type":"text","text":" is a four-star country estate hotel set on the shores of a picturesque lake near Cavan town. The property blends classic Georgian architecture with modern comfort, offering a serene retreat for relaxation, recreation, and exploration of the surrounding countryside. Its extensive grounds, woodlands, and water features make it a perfect destination for both leisure and outdoor enthusiasts."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes well-appointed rooms and suites with comfortable furnishings, modern amenities, and scenic views over the lake or gardens. Whether staying in a cosy room or a premium suite, guests can enjoy a blend of comfort, style, and peaceful surroundings."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Farnham Estate focuses on fresh, locally sourced ingredients, with elegant menus served in refined dining spaces overlooking the lake. Afternoon tea, casual lounges, and event facilities add to the estate’s appeal, making it suitable for both intimate stays and group gatherings."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The estate also offers a range of activities, including a championship golf course, fishing, walking trails, and spa facilities, allowing guests to combine relaxation with recreation."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its lakeside setting, elegant rooms, and variety of activities, Farnham Estate is especially suited to couples, families, golfers, and anyone seeking a tranquil yet engaging countryside escape in County Cavan."}]}]}

title: Luxury hotel by the sea and mountains
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury resort"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" Its unrivalled position at the foot of the Mourne Mountains with direct access to Royal County Down Golf Club, one of the world’s top-ranked courses."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Slieve Donard – Marine & Lawn Hotel & Resort"},{"type":"text","text":" is an iconic four-star retreat set on the stunning County Down coastline in the heart of Newcastle. Recently reimagined as part of the Marine & Lawn collection, the hotel blends Victorian grandeur with contemporary elegance, offering an inviting escape where mountain, forest, and sea meet. Its enviable location places guests steps from the beach, with panoramic views over the Irish Sea and sweeping vistas of the Mourne Mountains."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The hotel features a wide variety of beautifully designed rooms and suites, many with ocean or mountain outlooks, creating a calm and sophisticated base for both relaxation and adventure. Guests can explore landscaped grounds, stroll along the promenade, or simply take in the dramatic natural scenery that surrounds the property."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Slieve Donard is celebrated for its premium leisure facilities, including a newly refreshed spa with a pool overlooking the coastline, thermal experiences, and indulgent treatments inspired by the local landscape. The hotel also boasts several dining options showcasing regional produce, from relaxed coastal fare to elevated restaurant experiences."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its exceptional setting beside "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"italic"}],"text":"Royal County Down"},{"type":"text","text":", outdoor activities on its doorstep, and a perfect balance of heritage charm and modern luxury, Slieve Donard appeals to couples, golfers, wellness seekers, and anyone dreaming of a scenic coastal escape in one of the North of Ireland’s most breathtaking locations."}]}]}

title: Luxury Coastal Hotel
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 5-star luxury lodge"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" The only five-star property on the Causeway Coast, set beside Royal Portrush with panoramic views over the North Atlantic"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dunluce Lodge is a newly opened five-star retreat on Northern Ireland’s iconic Causeway Coast, positioned just steps from the fairways of Royal Portrush Golf Club. The lodge blends modern coastal luxury with understated elegance, offering spacious suites inspired by natural materials, soft muted tones, and expansive views of the rugged coastline. Its intimate size and peaceful setting make it a refined base for exploring the Giant’s Causeway, Portrush, and the region’s dramatic cliffs and beaches."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The lodge is also home to a boutique spa that embraces coastal-influenced wellness, with treatments inspired by sea minerals, sound therapy, and Irish skincare traditions. Guests can unwind in serene relaxation areas, enjoy fine dining that highlights local produce and fresh seafood, or simply take in the sweeping Atlantic views. Designed for travellers seeking privacy, comfort, and a high-end coastal escape, Dunluce Lodge brings a new level of luxury to one of Ireland’s most scenic regions."}]}]}

title: Spa Hotel in Antirm
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury resort"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" Its riverside Thermal Spa Village,  the only one of its kind in Ireland"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Galgorm Resort & Spa is a luxury four-star escape set in the peaceful County Antrim countryside, just a short drive from Belfast. Spread across a vast private estate, the resort combines contemporary luxury with natural beauty, offering a wide range of accommodation from elegant hotel rooms to riverside cabins, cottages, and forest lodges. With landscaped grounds, woodland paths, and the River Maine flowing through the centre of the property, it delivers a secluded and serene atmosphere ideal for relaxation."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The resort is best known for its award-winning Thermal Spa Village, where guests can unwind in outdoor hot tubs, hydrotherapy pools, saunas, steam rooms, and riverside relaxation areas. Alongside its exceptional spa facilities, Galgorm also offers multiple dining options, a championship golf course, and a variety of leisure activities on-site. It’s a perfect choice for couples, spa lovers, and anyone seeking a luxurious countryside break with a distinctive wellness focus."}]}]}

title: Elegant Countryside Manor
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star Rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What Makes It Unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" An elegant 18th-century country manor set within 200 acres of parkland, offering championship golf, tranquil woodland walks, and refined country-house charm just minutes from Waterford City."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Overview:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Faithlegg House is a beautifully restored Georgian manor that blends historic character with modern comfort. Set amid rolling countryside overlooking the River Suir Estuary, the estate offers a peaceful escape while remaining close to the vibrant culture and history of Waterford. Its stately façade, mature trees, and manicured grounds create a classic country retreat atmosphere."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Accommodation:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Rooms in the main house retain period elegance with warm tones, antique-style furnishings, and luxurious bedding. Contemporary suites and golf lodges offer additional space and modern design, ideal for couples, families, or extended stays."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Dining:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"The award-winning Roseville Rooms Restaurant showcases seasonal Irish produce in an intimate, candlelit setting. Guests can also enjoy relaxed dining and drinks in the lounge and bar, maintaining the welcoming country-house feel."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Golf & Leisure:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"The 18-hole championship golf course is a standout feature, attracting enthusiasts seeking scenic fairways and a challenging layout. Guests can also unwind in the leisure centre with pool, sauna, and spa treatments."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ideal For:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Golf enthusiasts, couples seeking a romantic countryside retreat, and travellers looking for refined comfort within easy reach of Waterford City and Ireland’s Ancient East."}]}]}

title: The prime view of the North Coast
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perched high above the rugged North Antrim coastline, Magheracross Viewpoint offers one of the most spectacular panoramas in Northern Ireland. Located between Portrush and Bushmills, this scenic stop provides sweeping views over Whiterocks Beach, Dunluce Castle, and the dramatic limestone cliffs that tumble down to the Atlantic Ocean. On a clear day, you can see right across to Donegal and even catch a glimpse of the Isle of Islay in Scotland."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A series of walkways and viewing platforms make it easy to take in the breathtaking scenery, with the sea crashing below and seabirds wheeling overhead. Whether you arrive at sunrise or sunset, the light across the cliffs and ocean creates a view that’s nothing short of magical."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: This is one of the key stops on a lot of bus tours so at times it can get very busy but still worth a visit!"}]}]}

title: Speciality Coffee & Eats
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Twilight Coffee & Bunkhouse in Carnlough is a charming, independent café and accommodation spot nestled along the Causeway Coastal Route in County Antrim. Located at 54-56 Harbour Road, it offers a warm and welcoming atmosphere with stunning views of Carnlough Harbour. The café serves specialty coffee from Bailies Coffee, along with a variety of teas, freshly baked goods, and a menu featuring gluten-free, vegetarian, and vegan options. It’s a perfect stop for travelers seeking a cosy place to relax and enjoy quality refreshments."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Opening Hours: (check online for updated)"}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"bulletList","content":[{"type":"listItem","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Wednesday, Thursday, Friday & Sunday: 9:00 AM – 4:30 PM (last kitchen orders at 3:00 PM)"}]}]},{"type":"listItem","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Saturday: 9:00 AM – 10:00 PM (kitchen closed from 3:00 PM – 5:00 PM; last kitchen orders at 8:30 PM)"}]}]},{"type":"listItem","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Monday & Tuesday: Closed"}]}]}]}]}

title: Hidden waterfall in the Glens
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just outside the village of Carnlough, a short, easy walk brings you to Cranny Falls, a lovely waterfall tucked into the hillside above the old limestone quarry. The trail starts near the harbour and follows a gentle incline along a well-marked path, passing through trees and old quarry workings that hint at the area’s industrial past."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"As you approach the top, the sound of water grows stronger before the falls come into view, a steady curtain of water dropping into a shaded pool, especially impressive after rain. There’s a viewing platform where you can take in the scene safely, and if you turn around, you’ll also get excellent views across Carnlough Bay and the surrounding countryside."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It’s a pleasant, accessible walk, about 20 minutes each way and a great way to see another side of the Antrim Coast, mixing natural beauty with a touch of local history."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note, this is a flat walk each way so suitable for all abilities!"}]}]}

title: Secluded Village waterfall
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just outside the village of Glenoe, this waterfall is one of the most photogenic waterfall spots in County Antrim. Glenoe Waterfall sits hidden in a tree-lined gorge, where the water tumbles down a series of rock ledges into a deep, shaded pool. It’s easily reached by a short path and a few steep steps, making it one of the most accessible waterfalls in the Glens."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The walk itself is short but atmospheric wooden bridge moss-covered rocks, and the constant sound of running water create a peaceful setting that feels far from the road above. After rainfall, the falls are particularly striking, with the spray catching the light through the trees."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Managed by the National Trust, Glenoe is well maintained, with viewing points and handrails that let you get close to the water without losing that wild, tucked-away feeling. It’s a perfect quick stop for anyone exploring the Antrim Coast, and a favourite with photographers and walkers alike."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Don’t just visit the waterfall, there is a picturesque small village just meters away that has an old world feel."}]}]}

title: Bistro, Butchers & Farm Shop
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"The Dairy, Gleno"},{"type":"text","text":" is a farm shop, bistro & café in a historic setting. It is situated at "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"5 Waterfall Road, Gleno"},{"type":"text","text":" The Dairy occupies the former village creamery and dairy,  a building whose history dates back to "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"1933"},{"type":"text","text":", with roots even earlier, in a cooperative creamery established 1914."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Inside, the café pays homage to its heritage: tables are crafted from old beams of the original dairy, a seat made from the former conveyor belt, rescued weighing scales and old windows form part of the refurbished interior. "}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Dairy blends rustic charm with a modern, welcoming atmosphere. The venue includes:"}]},{"type":"bulletList","content":[{"type":"listItem","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"farm-shop and butchers counter"},{"type":"text","text":", selling their own handmade meat products from the family’s farm — sausages, burgers, cuts of Irish beef and lamb. "}]}]},{"type":"listItem","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A café/bistro serving breakfast and lunch, using locally sourced produce, home-baked pastries, and a broad menu catering to meat eaters, vegetarians, vegans and coeliacs."}]}]},{"type":"listItem","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"On-site shop shelves filled with artisanal Northern Irish produce: chutneys, chocolates, cheeses, breads, sauces, sweets, plus The Dairy’s own coffee blend (roasted by a local roaster) and Gleno-valley honey. "}]}]}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"They also serve ice-cream from the revived local legacy of Maud's Ice Cream — Gleno was once home to the original Maud’s factory. "}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"There’s a "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"walled courtyard and outdoor seating"},{"type":"text","text":", including a covered marquee area — both dog-friendly, ideal for visitors enjoying the nearby countryside or a walk to the Gleno Waterfall."}]}]}

title: Scenic long sandy beach with cows
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Whitepark Bay is a long, pale-sand beach backed by dunes and low cliffs, great for long walks, dog runs and photos. You’ll park in a small lot above the bay and follow a short path with steps down to the sand, but facilities are minimal so bring water, snacks and layers. The sea can have strong tides and there is no lifeguard, so only swim if you know what you are doing and check tide times. Expect changeable, windy weather and wear sturdy shoes for walking on sand and uneven paths. "}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: there is often Grazing cows on the beach so this might be something you will love or hate! "}]}]}

title: Picturesque seaside village and beach
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Cushendun"},{"type":"text","text":" is a peaceful, picture-perfect coastal village on the Causeway Coast, set at the mouth of the River Dun and backed by the rolling hills of Glendun,  one of the nine Glens of Antrim. Designed in the early 1900s in a distinctive Cornish style, its whitewashed cottages, small harbour, and quiet beach give the village a unique charm. Managed by the National Trust, Cushendun is an ideal stop for a gentle stroll, sea views, and a slower pace of travel."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For food, "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Mary McBride’s"},{"type":"text","text":" is the village’s best-known spot, once famed as one of the smallest bars in Ireland. This cosy pub serves traditional dishes and fresh local seafood. It’s the perfect place to warm up after exploring the beach or wandering through the village."}]}]}

title: Dramatic Castle Location
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Dunluce Castle"},{"type":"text","text":" is one of the most dramatic historic sites on the Causeway Coast, perched on a sheer basalt cliff overlooking the North Atlantic. Dating back to the 13th century, the ruins of this once-powerful stronghold of the MacQuillan and later the MacDonnell clans are linked by a footbridge to the mainland, giving visitors a sense of its isolation and strategic importance. With sea caves below and sweeping views along the rugged coastline, it’s a place where history and landscape blend seamlessly."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A visit offers atmospheric courtyards, crumbling towers, and stories of battles, shipwrecks, and clan rivalries. Whether explored in moody mist or bright sunshine, Dunluce Castle delivers one of Northern Ireland’s most unforgettable coastal sights,  a perfect stop for travellers wanting history with spectacular scenery."}]}]}

title: Dramatic Headland and Castle ruins
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Kinbane Head"},{"type":"text","text":" is a rugged, windswept headland just outside Ballycastle, offering one of the most dramatic viewpoints on the Causeway Coast. Reached by a steep path, the promontory leads to the atmospheric ruins of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Kinbane Castle"},{"type":"text","text":", a 16th-century stronghold of the MacDonnell clan perched on a narrow limestone outcrop. The walk down and back up is steep but rewarded with sweeping views over Rathlin Island, the Antrim cliffs, and the crashing Atlantic below. Quiet, wild, and less visited than other coastal sites, Kinbane Head is a perfect stop for travellers seeking raw scenery, history, and a sense of solitude."}]}]}

title: Seaside town centre hotel
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star boutique hotel"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A freshly refurbished luxury coastal hotel in the heart of Portrush, combining maritime-inspired design with immediate access to beaches, restaurants, and Royal Portrush Golf Club"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Portrush Adelphi is a 4-star boutique hotel set right on Main Street in the centre of Portrush, placing guests steps from the town’s sandy beaches, harbour, and lively seaside atmosphere. Following a major refurbishment, the hotel offers contemporary, coastal-themed rooms with warm colours, modern finishes, and a relaxed sense of style. With its intimate scale and central location, it provides an ideal base for exploring the Causeway Coast, from the Giant’s Causeway to the dramatic Whiterocks shoreline."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Alongside its comfortable rooms, Portrush Adelphi features a stylish restaurant, the Red Sail Room, which focuses on fresh, seasonal and locally sourced produce, particularly seafood from the surrounding coast. Its close proximity to Royal Portrush Golf Club makes it especially appealing for golfers, while its central setting suits couples, families, and travellers seeking a walkable, relaxed luxury stay by the sea."}]}]}

title: Unique tour of the city
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lagan Adventures lets you swap asphalt for water by pedaling across the River Lagan on hydrobikes,  essentially water‑based bicycles that stay dry and stable as you ride. The launch point is beside Belfast's Waterfront Hall, giving you easy access from the city centre. As you pedal, you’ll drift past iconic riverside sights — including historic cranes, bridges, old industrial buildings, and the changing skyline — offering a refreshing and scenic way to experience Belfast on foot‑turned‑paddle‑turned‑pedal."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You don’t need to be a water sports pro (or even know how to swim) the hydrobikes are unsinkable and very stable, and safety gear is provided. The activity suits a wide range of people: solo explorers, couples, families (kids from around age 4 can join, using child‑seat bikes), or groups. Riders can choose a relaxed, gentle pace for sightseeing, or a more energetic pedal for a light workout. For extra novelty, there’s even an evening option — “Hydrobikes by Light” — where you glide under Belfast’s nighttime glow, the bikes illuminated gently as the cityscape reflects in the water."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" There’s no on‑site parking at the launch area, but parking is available a short walk away (e.g. around Lanyon Place or nearby transport hubs)"}]}]}

title: Old style luxury hotel
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star boutique retreat"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" Its centuries-old heritage and thatched-roof charm, combined with a modern, Nordic-inspired thermal spa experience."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"The Old Inn, Crawfordsburn"},{"type":"text","text":" is a four-star boutique escape nestled in the picturesque village of Crawfordsburn, just minutes from Belfast and the scenic North Down coastline. Steeped in history, this charming inn dates back to 1614 and beautifully blends its centuries-old architecture, complete with roaring fires, exposed beams, and rustic elegance, with contemporary comfort and understated luxury."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation ranges from cosy heritage rooms to beautifully appointed suites and signature lodges, each designed with warmth, texture, and craftsmanship at their heart. Surrounded by Crawfordsburn Country Park, the hotel offers an intimate, woodland-framed setting ideal for walkers, food lovers, and those seeking a quiet getaway rich in character."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Old Inn is home to a stylish restaurant serving elevated Irish cuisine, while its standout feature, the Treetop Spa, provides a serene, adults-only sanctuary overlooking the forest canopy. Guests can unwind in outdoor hot pools, saunas, and relaxation spaces, making it a perfect destination for indulgent downtime."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its blend of historic charm, boutique luxury, and tranquil woodland surroundings, The Old Inn appeals to couples, foodies, spa enthusiasts, and anyone looking for an atmospheric countryside escape that feels both timeless and uniquely Irish."}]}]}

title: National Trust property
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Mount Stewart, County Down"},{"type":"text","text":" is a stunning National Trust estate famous for its vibrant gardens, lakeside walks, and peaceful woodland trails. It’s perfect for gentle strolls, photography, or a relaxing day surrounded by history and nature. Parking is available at the main visitor car park, and clear paths lead through the formal gardens, around the lake, and into the surrounding woodlands."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Facilities include a café, toilets, and a visitor centre for a tour of the estate house, making it convenient for a longer visit, though it’s still wise to bring water, a jacket, and sturdy shoes, as some paths can be uneven or muddy after rain. The house itself is open for tours, offering a fascinating glimpse into the Stewart family’s history and beautifully preserved interiors."}]}]}

title: Forest Spa & Luxury Glamping Cabins
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" Luxury glamping-style retreat"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" Its elevated cabin hideaways overlooking the Mourne Mountains, offering an immersive, nature-first experience with hotel-style comfort."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"The Cabins at Enniskeen Estate"},{"type":"text","text":" offer a boutique countryside escape on a private 12-acre estate just outside Newcastle, County Down. Set on the edge of ancient woodland and framed by sweeping views of the Mourne Mountains, these luxury cabins provide a unique blend of rustic charm and modern comfort, creating an intimate hideaway perfect for nature lovers and couples seeking tranquillity."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Each cabin is thoughtfully designed with cosy interiors, plush beds, stylish furnishings, and floor-to-ceiling windows that bring the outdoors in. Private decks allow guests to enjoy peaceful mornings with mountain views, starlit evenings, and the soothing sounds of nature all around. With direct access to estate walking trails, wild meadows, and riverside paths, the setting delivers an idyllic backdrop for relaxation and adventure."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Guests can enjoy hearty breakfasts and locally inspired dining at the estate’s main house, or opt for outdoor firepits, picnic experiences, and quiet nights tucked away in their woodland retreat. The estate’s location also places visitors just minutes from Newcastle’s beaches, forest parks."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its secluded atmosphere, immersive natural setting, and cabin-style luxury, Enniskeen Estate appeals to hikers, romantic getaway seekers, and anyone looking to disconnect and experience the Mournes in a truly unique and peaceful way."}]}]}

title: Open Air history Museum
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ulster Folk and Transport Museum, County Down"},{"type":"text","text":" is an open-air museum that brings Ulster’s rural and industrial history to life. Set across expansive grounds in Cultra, it’s perfect for walking, exploring historic buildings, and learning about traditional crafts, farming, and transport through the ages. Visitors can wander between recreated villages, historic farmhouses, and a range of transport exhibits, including vintage trams and buses."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Parking is available on-site, and paths are mostly flat and easy to navigate, though some areas are outdoors on uneven ground, so sturdy shoes are recommended. Facilities include a café, picnic areas, toilets, and an indoor exhibition space, making it suitable for a full day out."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The museum is family-friendly, with interactive demonstrations, seasonal events, and plenty of space for children to run and explore. Dogs are welcome in outdoor areas on a lead but not inside buildings. Weather can change quickly, so layers and waterproofs are advisable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" It can get busy during school holidays and weekends, so early arrival is best for a quieter experience and to make the most of the outdoor exhibits."}]}]}

title: living symbol of peace
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"The Peace Maze, Castlewellan Forest Park"},{"type":"text","text":" is a unique outdoor attraction set within the park’s beautiful woodland. Designed as a living symbol of peace and reconciliation, it features over 6,000 yew trees forming a large, walkable labyrinth. It’s perfect for families, couples, or anyone looking for a quiet, reflective walk with a sense of adventure."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Parking is available at Castlewellan Forest Park, and the paths through the maze are mostly level but can be uneven in places, so comfortable shoes are recommended. The maze can take 20–40 minutes to complete depending on your pace, and there are plenty of spots along the way to pause and enjoy the surrounding forest scenery."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Facilities at the park include toilets, a café, picnic areas, and walking trails, but the maze itself is outdoors with no additional amenities inside, so bring water, layers, and snacks if needed. Dogs are allowed on leads throughout the park but not inside the maze if it’s busy."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The maze is best enjoyed during quieter times, as weekends and holidays can be busy. Weather can change quickly, so be prepared for rain or wind when visiting."}]}]}

title: Official Show Tour
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Game of Thrones Studio Tour "},{"type":"text","text":" is an immersive experience that takes fans behind the scenes of the iconic TV series. Set in purpose-built studio spaces, the tour showcases authentic sets, props, costumes, and special effects used throughout the show, giving visitors a hands-on look at how Westeros came to life."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Parking is available on-site via bus shuttle, and the tour is mostly indoors with flat, accessible paths, making it suitable for most visitors. Facilities include a café, toilets, and a gift shop with exclusive merchandise. Guided and self-guided options are available, and the experience typically takes 1.5–2 hours."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The tour is family-friendly, though some areas may be dark or contain special effects. Photography is allowed in most areas, and staff are on hand to answer questions and enhance the experience. Comfortable shoes are recommended if you plan to explore the studio and surrounding areas fully."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The tour is very popular, especially on weekends and during school holidays, so booking in advance is strongly recommended to avoid disappointment."}]}]}

title: Elevated viewpoint of the lough
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Flagstaff Viewpoint lets you swap busy streets for big skies, offering one of the most dramatic panoramic stops in County Armagh. Set high on the edge of the Ring of Gullion near Newry, this roadside viewpoint opens up sweeping views across Carlingford Lough, the Mourne Mountains, the Cooley Peninsula, and on a clear day, as far as the Irish Sea. It’s an easy, rewarding way to experience the region’s natural beauty without committing to a long hike."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"From the viewpoint, you can linger at the stone viewing area, take photos, or simply soak in the scale of the landscape as light and weather shift across land and water. Informational panels help you identify landmarks and understand the area’s geography, making it as interesting as it is scenic. Whether you stop for five minutes or half an hour, it’s a place that invites you to pause and look a little longer."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You don’t need to be an experienced walker or outdoor enthusiast to enjoy Flagstaff — it’s accessible, family-friendly, and suitable for all ages. Couples, solo travellers, coach groups, and families often include it as a highlight on a drive through South Armagh. For those who want to stretch their legs, nearby forest paths and trails offer optional short walks with equally impressive views."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For a different atmosphere, visiting in the early morning or near sunset adds extra drama, as low light catches the lough and mountains in soft colour, making it a favourite stop for photographers and sunset-chasers alike."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" There is a dedicated lay-by car park right at the viewpoint, making access easy. The area can be exposed and windy, so warm layers are recommended even on mild days."}]}]}

title: Scenic drive, walk and park
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Slieve Gullion Forest Park and Scenic Drive lets you trade everyday roads for one of North of  Ireland’s most atmospheric mountain landscapes. Located at the heart of the Ring of Gullion in County Armagh, this forest park wraps around the lower slopes of Slieve Gullion, blending woodland, open views, and myth-rich scenery into an easy, rewarding outdoor escape."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The forest park offers a mix of short, well-maintained walking trails suitable for all ages and abilities. Families are especially well catered for, with play areas, picnic spots, and gentle paths that wind through trees and alongside streams. You don’t need to be a seasoned hiker, most routes are relaxed and clearly marked but those looking for more of a challenge can connect onto longer trails that climb higher into the landscape."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For those who prefer to explore from the car, the Slieve Gullion Scenic Drive is a highlight in its own right. The narrow mountain road loops around the slopes, revealing changing viewpoints across South Armagh, Carlingford Lough, and the Cooley Mountains. Frequent lay-bys allow you to stop safely, take photos, or simply enjoy the quiet and scale of the surroundings."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The area is steeped in local legend, with Slieve Gullion closely tied to Irish mythology, adding an extra layer of intrigue to your visit. Seasonal changes bring different moods,  spring wildflowers, lush summer greens, autumn colour, or misty winter drama, making it worth visiting more than once."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Car parking is available within Slieve Gullion Forest Park, with facilities including toilets and picnic areas. The scenic drive is narrow in places and best taken slowly. Comfortable footwear and weather-appropriate clothing are recommended, as conditions can change quickly on the mountain if you decide to hike the peak."}]}]}

title: Granduer in Irelands Faith centre
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"St Patrick’s Roman Catholic Cathedral rises above Armagh with unmistakable grandeur, offering a powerful blend of history, faith, and architectural drama. Perched on a hill overlooking the city, the cathedral is one of Ireland’s most impressive ecclesiastical buildings and a defining landmark of County Armagh."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Built in the 19th century in a striking Gothic Revival style, the cathedral immediately draws the eye with its twin spires, richly detailed stonework, and commanding presence. Step inside and the scale becomes even more impressive, soaring ceilings, intricate stained-glass windows, colourful mosaics, and finely carved altars create a space that feels both monumental and intimate. Even for non-religious visitors, the craftsmanship and atmosphere make it a rewarding place to explore."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You don’t need specialist knowledge to enjoy the cathedral. Visitors are welcome to wander quietly, sit and reflect, or simply admire the artistry and sense of calm. It suits solo travellers, couples, families, and tour groups alike, and is often combined with a wider walk around Armagh’s historic streets, which are rich in ecclesiastical heritage."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For a different experience, visiting during a service allows you to hear the cathedral’s renowned acoustics and organ, while evening light can cast dramatic colours through the stained glass, giving the interior a softer, more contemplative mood."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The cathedral is free to enter, though donations are welcomed. Limited parking is available nearby, with additional parking in the city centre a short walk away. As it is an active place of worship, visitors are asked to dress respectfully and be mindful of services in progress."}]}]}

title: slice of literary history
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Armagh Robinson Library offers a step back into the world of 18th-century scholarship, providing one of the most unique and atmospheric cultural experiences in the city. Founded in 1771 by Archbishop Richard Robinson, this historic library stands just a short walk from Armagh’s cathedrals and remains one of the oldest public libraries in Ireland."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Inside, the library feels more like a museum of knowledge than a modern reading room. Original wooden bookcases line the walls from floor to ceiling, filled with rare and early printed books, manuscripts, and maps that reflect centuries of learning in theology, science, literature, and history. Highlights include early editions of classic works, beautifully illustrated volumes, and fascinating artefacts that bring the past vividly to life."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You don’t need to be a historian to enjoy Robinson Library. Visitors can explore the exhibition spaces, admire the craftsmanship of the building, and learn about the ideas and people that shaped intellectual life in Armagh and beyond. It suits solo visitors, couples, and small groups, and pairs especially well with a relaxed cultural stroll through the city."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The experience is calm and reflective, encouraging you to slow down and take in the detail, from the smell of old books to the quiet creak of the floors. For those interested in heritage, literature, or simply unusual places, it’s a memorable stop."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Entry is free, though numbers may be limited at busy times. Opening hours can vary, so it’s best to check in advance. There is limited on-street parking nearby, with additional parking available in Armagh city centre, a short walk away."}]}]}

title: Church of Ireland Centre
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"St Patrick’s Church of Ireland Cathedral stands at the heart of Armagh’s historic identity, offering a deep sense of continuity, tradition, and quiet grandeur. Set on a hilltop in the city centre, it is one of the most important ecclesiastical sites in Ireland and is recognised as the spiritual home of the Church of Ireland."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With origins stretching back to the 5th century, the cathedral occupies the site traditionally associated with St Patrick himself. While much of the current structure dates from later medieval periods, the building retains a timeless presence, with solid stone walls, narrow windows, and a simple, dignified elegance.
