The walk takes around 30 minutes each way and crosses uneven, often boggy ground, but the changing perspectives along the route make it well worth the effort."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Horn Head is ideal for walkers, photographers, and anyone who loves dramatic coastal scenery. The panoramic views take in rugged cliffs, offshore islands, and the constantly shifting colours of the sea, creating a powerful sense of scale and isolation that lingers long after you leave."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For the best experience, clear days offer incredible visibility and sharp coastal detail, while windier conditions bring crashing waves and a raw, exhilarating atmosphere. Either way, the scenery never disappoints."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The area is very exposed, and strong winds are common, so warm layers and sturdy footwear are essential. Take care near cliff edges and allow extra time if conditions are challenging."}]}]}

title: Wilderness, Castle, and Lough
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Escape into the wild heart of Donegal at "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Glenveagh National Park"},{"type":"text","text":", a vast and unspoilt landscape of rugged mountains, mirror-like lakes, and peaceful woodlands. This remote-feeling park offers a rare sense of space and calm, making it an ideal destination for anyone seeking nature, history, and quiet beauty in equal measure."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"From the visitor centre, a scenic and mostly flat walk follows the shoreline of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Lough Veagh"},{"type":"text","text":", taking around 40 minutes to reach the enchanting "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Glenveagh Castle"},{"type":"text","text":". Along the way, the path offers constant views across the water to the surrounding Derryveagh Mountains, creating a gentle and immersive introduction to the park. For those who prefer to conserve energy, a convenient shuttle bus runs from the car park directly to the castle."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The 19th-century castle and its beautifully maintained gardens feel like a hidden oasis, sheltered by woodland and mountains. Beyond the castle, Glenveagh offers a network of walking trails suited to all abilities, from short, leisurely strolls to longer hikes deep into the wilderness."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Glenveagh National Park is perfect for walkers, photographers, families, and anyone looking to slow down and reconnect with nature. Whether you’re wandering the gardens, walking by the lake, or exploring the wider park, it’s a place of profound peace and breathtaking scenery."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Weather can change quickly in the mountains, so dressing in layers and wearing comfortable footwear is recommended. During peak seasons, arriving earlier in the day can make for a quieter and more relaxed visit."}]}]}

title: Donegal's Roadside Cascade
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just a short drive from Ardara, you'll find the stunning Assaranca Waterfall,  "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Assaranca Waterfall"},{"type":"text","text":" (also known as Eas a’ Ranca) offers a striking natural spectacle where mountain water meets the Atlantic breeze. Unlike hidden woodland falls, this waterfall is dramatic and open, tumbling down a rocky cliff face just moments from the roadside, making it both accessible and unforgettable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Fed by the nearby hills, the waterfall flows strongest after rainfall, when water cascades powerfully over dark stone and spreads into multiple streams. The surrounding landscape feels wild and elemental, with open views toward the coast and the constant sound of rushing water adding to the atmosphere."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Assaranca Waterfall is ideal for photographers, short-stop explorers, and anyone touring the Wild Atlantic Way. You don’t need a long walk to enjoy it, but taking a few moments to explore the rocks and viewpoints allows you to fully appreciate the scale and movement of the falls against Donegal’s raw scenery."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For the best experience, visiting after heavy rain brings the waterfall to life, while quieter mornings offer a more peaceful moment to take in the surroundings. The changing light and weather ensure no two visits feel the same."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Rocks around the waterfall can be slippery, especially in wet conditions, so sturdy footwear and caution are advised when exploring nearby viewpoints."}]}]}

title: A Hidden Coastal Gem
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away along the rugged coastline of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Glencolumbkille"},{"type":"text","text":", "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Lug na Druan Pier"},{"type":"text","text":" is a true hidden gem that captures the wild spirit of Donegal at its most dramatic. Remote and largely untouched, a viewpoint of the cliffs where land gives way to the full force of the Atlantic."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Standing here, you can feel and hear the raw power of the ocean as waves crash against towering cliffs below. The views are vast and uninterrupted, stretching endlessly along the coastline and out across the open sea. Many consider this spot to offer "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"some of the very best views in all of Donegal"},{"type":"text","text":", and it’s easy to see why—the scale, isolation, and intensity of the landscape are unforgettable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lug na Druan Pier is ideal for quiet reflection, photography, or simply pausing to take in the untamed beauty of the coast. There’s a profound sense of connection to nature here, heightened by the absence of crowds and the ever-changing moods of the ocean and sky."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For the most powerful experience, visit on a clear day when the light sharpens the cliffs and the sea stretches endlessly to the horizon. Even in rougher weather, the drama of the waves and wind makes this a place that leaves a lasting impression."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The area is exposed with steep cliffs nearby, so caution is essential, especially in windy or wet conditions. Sturdy footwear and an awareness of changing tides and weather will help ensure a safe and enjoyable visit."}]}]}

title: Ireland's Highest Sea Cliffs
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Experience some of the most breathtaking coastal scenery in Ireland at "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Slieve League"},{"type":"text","text":", home to some of the highest sea cliffs in Europe and rising to almost three times the height of the Cliffs of Moher. Towering above the Atlantic, these vast cliffs create an overwhelming sense of scale and drama that leaves a lasting impression from the moment you arrive."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visitors can walk or take a convenient shuttle bus to the main viewing area, where well-positioned viewpoints offer sweeping views along the cliff edge and far out across the ocean. Even from these accessible points, the scenery is nothing short of spectacular, with sheer drops, crashing waves, and endless horizons."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For those seeking a deeper adventure, the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Pilgrim’s Path"},{"type":"text","text":" provides a strenuous but incredibly rewarding hike. This historic route follows a narrow, exposed trail across the cliffs, delivering unforgettable panoramas and an exhilarating sense of height. It’s an experience best suited to confident walkers with a good head for heights."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Slieve League is ideal for photographers, walkers, and anyone eager to experience Donegal’s wild Atlantic coastline at its most dramatic. Whether enjoying the views from the main lookout or tackling the Pilgrim’s Path, it’s a place that truly showcases the raw beauty of Ireland’s west coast."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Weather conditions can change quickly, and cliffs are exposed, so sturdy footwear and caution are essential. On busy days, arriving early can help you enjoy a quieter and more relaxed visit."}]}]}

title: Donegal's Heavenly Glen
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked at the base of the mighty "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Mount Errigal"},{"type":"text","text":", the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Poisoned Glen"},{"type":"text","text":" or "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"italic"}],"text":"the Heavenly Glen"},{"type":"text","text":", as locals will tell you it was originally meant to be called, is one of Donegal’s most dramatic and atmospheric landscapes. Steep-sided mountains rise sharply from the valley floor, creating a powerful sense of scale and a setting that feels both wild and otherworldly."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The glen offers a range of walking options for those eager to explore. A rough but rewarding "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"4km trail"},{"type":"text","text":" leads into the heart of the valley, following rugged ground and opening up striking views of Errigal and the surrounding peaks. For more adventurous hikers, longer, more strenuous routes branch out across boggy, untamed terrain, providing multi-hour hikes and truly unforgettable vistas deep in the Donegal wilderness."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Poisoned Glen is ideal for experienced walkers, photographers, and anyone drawn to raw, dramatic scenery. Whether you’re taking on a shorter walk or committing to a longer hike, the atmosphere here feels intense and immersive, with shifting light and weather constantly transforming the landscape."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For the best experience, clear days reveal the full grandeur of the mountains, while mist and cloud add a moody, almost mystical quality to the glen. Either way, it’s a place that leaves a strong impression."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Trails can be rough, wet, and unmarked in places, so sturdy footwear, navigation skills, and appropriate clothing are essential. Conditions can change quickly, particularly higher up, so planning ahead is advised."}]}]}

title: Historic Clifftop Fortress
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perched on a rugged headland overlooking "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Lough Swilly"},{"type":"text","text":", "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Fort Dunree"},{"type":"text","text":" is a fascinating blend of history and coastal scenery. This well-preserved military fort offers a glimpse into Ireland’s past, with coastal defenses that once guarded one of the country’s most strategic sea inlets. The combination of historic structures, museum exhibits, and sweeping views makes it a highlight for both history enthusiasts and nature lovers."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visitors can wander along the old gun emplacements, explore the small museum detailing the fort’s role from the 18th century through World War II, and take in panoramic views across the lough toward the Inishowen Peninsula. The rugged coastline, sparkling waters, and distant hills create a striking backdrop that’s both dramatic and peaceful."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Fort Dunree is ideal for families, walkers, and anyone keen to combine history with the natural beauty of Donegal. The site’s elevated position makes it perfect for photography, and the walking trails around the fort allow you to soak in the scenery at a leisurely pace."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The site is open seasonally, and weather can be windy and changeable, so warm layers and sturdy footwear are recommended for a comfortable visit."}]}]}

title: Ancient Fort with Epic Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perched atop "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Greenan Mountain"},{"type":"text","text":" near Burt, the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Grianán of Aileach"},{"type":"text","text":" is one of Ireland’s most iconic ancient stone forts. Dating back over a thousand years, this circular stone fortress offers a fascinating glimpse into early medieval life and the strategic importance of this site, which once served as a royal stronghold for the Kings of Aileach."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The fort itself is remarkably well-preserved, with thick stone walls and a commanding position that provides "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"panoramic views over Inishowen, Lough Swilly, and beyond"},{"type":"text","text":". On a clear day, the vistas stretch across Donegal and even toward County Derry, making it one of the most spectacular lookout points in the region."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visitors can walk around the outer ramparts, climb the interior steps, and imagine the lives of the people who once defended this hilltop fortress. The surrounding area also offers gentle walking trails and open fields, making it a peaceful spot to explore after taking in the historic site."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Grianán of Aileach is ideal for history enthusiasts, photographers, and anyone drawn to dramatic landscapes and ancient monuments. The combination of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"heritage, elevation, and sweeping coastal and countryside views"},{"type":"text","text":" makes it a must-see when exploring Donegal."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The site is fully accessible on foot, but weather can be exposed and windy at the summit, so sturdy footwear and warm layers are recommended."}]}]}

title: Historic Seaside Fortress
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked on the edge of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Sheephaven Bay"},{"type":"text","text":" near Creeslough, "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Doe Castle"},{"type":"text","text":" is a striking example of a medieval tower house set against the wild beauty of Donegal’s coastline. Dating back to the 15th century, the castle has a rich history, having served as a stronghold for the McSwyne clan and later as a strategic site in the region’s turbulent past."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The castle is remarkably well-preserved, with its stone walls, corner towers, and moat offering a tangible sense of its defensive purpose. Visitors can explore the interior and ramparts, imagining life in a bygone era, while the surrounding grounds provide a serene setting overlooking the sheltered waters of the bay."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Doe Castle is ideal for history enthusiasts, families, and photographers. Its combination of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"historic architecture and scenic coastal backdrop"},{"type":"text","text":" makes it one of Donegal’s most picturesque and accessible castles. Walking along the bay or exploring the castle grounds, you can enjoy both the cultural significance and the natural beauty of the area."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The castle is open seasonally, and while much of the site is easily accessible, care should be taken on uneven stone surfaces. Weather can be changeable, so warm clothing and sturdy footwear are recommended."}]}]}

title: Iconic Beach Shipwreck
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked along the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"sweeping sands of Magheraclogher Beach"},{"type":"text","text":" near Bunbeg in west Donegal, "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Bád Eddie"},{"type":"text","text":" (literally "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"italic"}],"text":"Eddie’s Boat"},{"type":"text","text":" in Irish) is an iconic and atmospheric shipwreck that has become a beloved landmark of the Gaelic coast. What began in the 1970s as a grounded fishing vessel—originally named "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"italic"}],"text":"Cara na Mara"},{"type":"text","text":" or "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"italic"}],"text":"Friend of the Sea"},{"type":"text","text":"—has weathered decades of Atlantic winds and tides, its timber slowly eroding yet somehow still clinging to the strand."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Over time the wreck has grown into something much more than a rusting old boat. With "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Mount Errigal rising in the background"},{"type":"text","text":", it has become one of the most photographed scenes in Donegal, featured in music videos and used as a backdrop for wedding portraits and creative photography. Walking along the beach, you’ll feel the pull of this dramatic coastline—where sea, sky, and land meet in ever‑changing light—and see why Bád Eddie holds a special place in both local memory and the imagination of visitors."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The wreck is fragile and its remains are deteriorating, so caution is advised if you explore close up. Efforts by the local community aim to preserve its legacy for future generations."}]}]}

title: Traditional Irish Pub Experience
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nestled on the stunning Wild Atlantic Drive, you'll find The Singing Pub, a quintessential Irish cottage with a traditional thatched roof and glowing turf fires inside. It's the kind of place where you can settle in for a quiet pint or enjoy a hearty meal of fresh, local seafood. The atmosphere is always welcoming, with live traditional music often filling the air, making it a beloved spot for both locals and those of us just passing through."}]}]}

title: Cozy Pub with Bay Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Settle in for a perfect pint at this quintessential Irish pub, a beloved local spot right on the shores of Sheephaven Bay. You'll find a warm welcome from the friendly staff and a cozy atmosphere, especially with the turf fire crackling away. Grab a seat on the patio to soak in the incredible coastal views, and be sure to try the fresh, locally sourced seafood, it's what they're famous for."}]}]}

title: Seaside Cafe with Stunning Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Right on the shores of Lough Swilly, this is the perfect spot to relax and soak in some incredible sea views. It's a favourite for both locals and visitors, offering everything from a hearty breakfast to a tasty lunch, with plenty of fresh, local ingredients. Grab a seat on the glass patio if you can; the panoramic view of the coastline is absolutely breathtaking, making it a perfect backdrop for a casual meal or a coffee with friends."}]}]}

title: Hike Donegal's Highest Peak
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Rising proudly above the central valleys of Donegal, "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Mount Errigal"},{"type":"text","text":" is Donegal’s tallest peak at 751 meters and one of the most iconic mountains in Ireland. Its distinctive "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"pale quartzite summit"},{"type":"text","text":" catches the light in a way that makes it visible for miles, giving it a striking, almost otherworldly presence against the rugged backdrop of the Derryveagh Mountains."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Errigal is a magnet for hikers and adventure seekers. The most popular route is the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"steep, well-trodden path from the car park at Gartan"},{"type":"text","text":", which climbs directly to the summit in roughly 2–3 hours. The terrain is challenging, with loose scree near the top, but the effort is richly rewarded: sweeping "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"panoramic views"},{"type":"text","text":" of the surrounding mountains, valleys, and on a clear day, the glinting waters of the Atlantic and Donegal’s coastline."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For photographers and nature lovers, Errigal’s shifting light and cloud patterns make each ascent feel unique. Sunrise and sunset are particularly spectacular, casting dramatic shadows across the jagged peaks and valleys below."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" The climb is steep and exposed in places, so sturdy footwear, warm clothing, and a good level of fitness are recommended. Weather on the mountain can change rapidly, so it’s important to check conditions before setting out."}]}]}

title: Lakeside Luxury in Donegal
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury country estate"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":" "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A lakeside retreat combining elegant accommodation, award-winning dining, outdoor activities, and stunning views over Lough Erne in scenic Donegal countryside."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Harvey’s Point"},{"type":"text","text":" is a four-star luxury country estate hotel set on the shores of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Lough Erne"},{"type":"text","text":" near Donegal town. The property blends classic lakeside charm with modern comfort, offering a serene retreat for relaxation, fine dining, and exploration of the surrounding landscape. Its beautifully maintained gardens, walking paths, and waterside location make it ideal for both leisure and outdoor enthusiasts."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes elegantly appointed rooms and suites with tasteful décor, modern amenities, and panoramic views over the lake or gardens. Whether staying in a cosy room or a premium suite, guests enjoy a peaceful and stylish setting that enhances the tranquil lakeside atmosphere."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Harvey’s Point focuses on fresh, locally sourced ingredients, with refined menus served in dining rooms overlooking "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Lough Erne"},{"type":"text","text":". Guests can enjoy fine dining, casual meals, or afternoon tea, while elegant lounges and event facilities cater to both intimate stays and larger gatherings."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The estate also offers a variety of activities, including walking trails, fishing, watersports, and spa facilities, allowing visitors to combine relaxation with recreation in a stunning natural setting."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its lakeside location, luxurious rooms, and wide range of activities, Harvey’s Point is especially suited to couples, families, and anyone seeking a peaceful yet indulgent escape in County Donegal."}]}]}

title: Handcrafted Donegal Mountain Escape
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Escape to a place of true tranquility at Neadú, which beautifully translates to 'nestle' in Irish. Tucked away with breathtaking views of the Derryveagh mountains, you'll find a collection of unique, handcrafted tiny homes perfect for a peaceful retreat. Whether you choose the Birdbox treehouse or the earth-sheltered Hide, you're guaranteed a cozy stay with a wood-burning stove and a front-row seat to stunning sunrises. It’s the ideal quiet base for exploring the wild beauty of County Donegal."}]}]}

title: High-Speed Coastal Sea Safari
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get ready for an exhilarating adventure on the "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Wild Atlantic Way"},{"type":"text","text":" with a high-speed boat tour departing from "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Burtonport Pier"},{"type":"text","text":". From the moment you set off, the Atlantic energy is unmistakable as the boat skims across the waves, opening up a thrilling new perspective on Donegal’s coastline."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The tour explores the rugged waters around "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Arranmore Island"},{"type":"text","text":" and its neighbouring islands, weaving past dramatic sea cliffs, rocky outcrops, and hidden sea caves that can only be reached from the water. The constantly changing coastline, shaped by wind and waves, feels raw and untamed, making every turn of the journey exciting."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Wildlife sightings are a highlight of the experience. Dolphins are often spotted riding the bow wave, while seals, gannets, and other seabirds are regularly seen along the cliffs and islands. Throughout the tour, a knowledgeable local skipper shares stories of the area’s maritime history, island life, and wildlife, adding depth and context to the journey."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"This experience is ideal for adventure seekers, photographers, and anyone wanting to see Donegal from a fresh and unforgettable angle. It’s a fast-paced, immersive way to experience the sheer scale and beauty of the Atlantic coast."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Note:"},{"type":"text","text":" Tours are weather dependent and can be bumpy in rough conditions. Warm, waterproof clothing is recommended, and advance booking is advised during busy periods."}]}]}

title: Ireland in Stone
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Stones of Ireland, A large stone map of Ireland, called Clocha na hÉireann, where each county is carved from its own native stone by a mason from that county. It was created to commemorate the 1916 Easter Rising and is located in the car park opposite the Glencolumbkille Folk Village."}]}]}

title: Self-Guided Donegal Bike Tours
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ireland by Bike"},{"type":"text","text":" is a family-run cycling tour company based in Carrick, County Donegal, offering self-guided cycling experiences along the Wild Atlantic Way and through some of the county’s most scenic backroads. With tailored routes, e-bike and hybrid bike hire, and full support including luggage transfers and navigation, it allows cyclists of all abilities to explore Donegal at their own pace. It’s an ideal option for active travellers looking to combine outdoor adventure with breathtaking coastal and countryside scenery."}]}]}

title: Wild Atlantic Dune Walk
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Five Finger Strand is a wild and windswept beach on the Inishowen Peninsula, framed by some of Europe's highest sand dunes. It's named for five sea stacks that resemble fingers. While swimming is unsafe due to strong currents, it's a spectacular spot for a walk."}]}]}

title: A Stunning Hidden Gem
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":" One of Inishowen's hidden gems, Tullagh Strand, a stunning horseshoe-shaped bay with a vast expanse of golden sand. It's a perfect spot for a long, refreshing walk, and you'll often see windsurfers and wing foilers dancing on the waves. The beach is easily reached from the grassy car park right behind the dunes, so you'll be feeling the sand between your toes in no time."}]}]}

title: A Wild Atlantic Cove
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You'll find this little gem tucked away on the stunning Rosguill Peninsula, a perfect stop along the Atlantic Drive. It's a small, wild cove where you can spend ages just watching the powerful Atlantic waves crash against the rocks. From the sand, you get a gorgeous view across the water towards Downings. It's a quiet spot, ideal for soaking in the sea air and the raw beauty of Donegal, located right by a Wild Atlantic Way viewpoint and car park."}]}]}

title: Remote Coastal Hike & Beach
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":" Donegal's most stunning beach gems, a secluded bay with golden sands and dramatic sea caves. Officially known as Boyeeghter Bay, reaching this spot is part of the experience, involving a rewarding 25-minute hike over a steep, gravel path from the car park, which requires a €5 fee. Once you arrive, the raw beauty is breathtaking, but be warned: the currents are extremely dangerous, so swimming is strictly off-limits. For the best experience, time your visit with the low tide to explore the incredible sea cave and the full expanse of the beach."}]}]}

title: Donegal's Hidden Coastal Gem
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A stunning little cove with crystal-clear water perfect for a swim, kayak, or even a bit of pier jumping. It's a beautiful spot for a walk, with powdery white sand and dunes to explore, offering gorgeous views of the nearby islands."}]}]}

title: Secluded Horseshoe Bay Beach
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away at the tip of the Slieve League Peninsula, you'll find our stunning Silver Strand, a true hidden gem. It's a bit of a journey, but once you arrive at the car park and see the horseshoe bay below, you'll know it was worth it. To reach the sand, you'll need to take the winding staircase of about 170 steps down the cliffside, which takes just a few minutes. The beach itself is sheltered by the dramatic cliffs, making it a perfect sun trap with crystal-clear water, ideal for a brisk swim on a calm day."}]}]}

title: Coastal Walks & Mountain Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just North of Newcastle, Dundrum Bay is one of the most picturesque coastal spots in County Down. The wide, sandy bay curves gently into the Irish Sea, framed by the dramatic Mourne Mountains, creating a stunning backdrop for walkers, photographers, and nature lovers alike."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The bay is dotted with dunes, rocky outcrops, and salt marshes, and is home to a variety of wildlife, including seabirds, waders, and seals. It’s a favourite spot for watersports such as windsurfing and kiteboarding, especially when the wind picks up across the open water."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"At the head of the bay sits the historic Dundrum Castle, a Norman ruin perched on a hillside, offering sweeping views of the coast. Coastal paths and trails weave along the shoreline, making it easy to explore the natural beauty without ever leaving the village."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dundrum Bay is perfect for a peaceful stroll, a picnic, or simply soaking up the wild, windswept scenery. The bay’s combination of mountains, sea, and history makes it a must-see stop for anyone exploring County Down."}]}]}

title: Hilltop Castle with Panoramic Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perched on a hill overlooking Dundrum Bay, Dundrum Castle is a striking Norman ruin with a dramatic coastal backdrop. Built in the 12th century, the castle was once a key defensive stronghold, and today its crumbling stone walls and towers give a glimpse into Ireland’s medieval past."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visitors can wander among the ruins, exploring the remaining gatehouse, ramparts, and towers while taking in panoramic views of the Irish Sea and the Mourne Mountains. The climb up to the castle is short but steep, rewarding visitors with a sense of history and a breathtaking vantage point over the bay."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The castle’s windswept location makes it especially atmospheric at sunrise or sunset, and it’s a favourite spot for photographers and history enthusiasts. Though largely in ruins, Dundrum Castle retains a wild, untamed charm that feels both remote and timeless."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nearby Dundrum village offers a few amenities and makes a perfect base for exploring the castle, bay, and coastal trails."}]}]}

title: Historic Coastal Village & Walk
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked along the eastern coast of County Down, Kearney is a small, tranquil village with a timeless, coastal charm. Nestled between rocky cliffs and sandy coves, the village is surrounded by sweeping views of the Irish Sea, making it a peaceful retreat for walkers, photographers, and anyone wanting to escape the busier towns nearby."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The village itself is quiet but picturesque, with traditional cottages and narrow lanes that hint at its historic past. Just a short stroll from the centre, you’ll find small beaches and rugged shoreline perfect for exploring rock pools, watching seabirds, or simply enjoying the sound of waves."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Kearney is also known for its fishing heritage, with a few remnants of old piers and slipways that speak to its maritime history. Coastal walks connect Kearney to nearby bays and villages, offering visitors a chance to take in the dramatic cliffs, dunes, and sea vistas that make this part of County Down so special."}]}]}

title: Royal Residence & Gardens
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set in the heart of County Down, Hillsborough Castle is an elegant Georgian mansion surrounded by beautifully landscaped gardens. Serving as the official residence of the British monarch in North of Ireland and as the residence of the Secretary of State, the castle combines history, politics, and grandeur in one stunning location."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The castle grounds are filled with manicured lawns, formal gardens, and wooded walks, perfect for a peaceful stroll or photography. Visitors can explore parts of the castle interior on guided tours, seeing lavish rooms, historic art, and period furnishings that showcase centuries of British and Irish history."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Hillsborough Castle is not only a centre of heritage but also a place of calm beauty. The gardens change with the seasons, offering colourful displays in spring and summer, golden foliage in autumn, and a serene, frosty landscape in winter. It’s an ideal stop for those interested in history, architecture, or simply a quiet escape in a historic setting."}]}]}

title: Iconic Hilltop Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Rising high above Newtownards, Scrabo Tower is one of County Down’s most iconic landmarks. Perched on Scrabo Hill, this tall stone monument overlooks Strangford Lough, the surrounding countryside, and the North Down coast, creating a dramatic and memorable view."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Built in the 19th century as a memorial to Charles Stewart, the 3rd Marquess of Londonderry, the tower’s turreted, Scottish Baronial design gives it a striking silhouette visible for miles. The climb up Scrabo Hill is atmospheric, with paths winding through wooded areas and past old quarries, leading to sweeping views that feel far removed from everyday life."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"While the tower itself is sometimes closed, the surrounding Scrabo Country Park is open to visitors year-round. Its walking trails, picnic spots, and panoramic vistas make it a favourite for photographers, walkers, and history enthusiasts. On a clear day, the view from the hill captures the bay, distant hills, and countryside, making it one of the most scenic spots in County Down."}]}]}

title: Ancient Monastic Hilltop Escape
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Located on Mahee Island in Strangford Lough, Nendrum is one of Northern Ireland’s most intriguing early Christian sites. Thought to have been founded in the 5th century by St Mochaoi, the monastic settlement thrived beside the lough’s calm waters for centuries."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The site features three concentric stone enclosures, the ruins of a church, a graveyard, a sundial, and the remains of a round tower. These ancient foundations give visitors a real sense of the layout and scale of this early monastic community. One of Nendrum’s most remarkable features is its early tidal mill, an ingenious example of medieval engineering that once harnessed the waters of the lough."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A short walk along the causeway to Mahee Island feels like stepping back in time. Surrounded by views across the lough and to the distant shore, the site has a peaceful, reflective atmosphere. It’s a favourite spot for history enthusiasts, photographers, and walkers who want to combine archaeology with the natural beauty of Strangford Lough."}]}]}

title: A Tale of Two Castles
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set near Strangford, Castle Ward is a unique and atmospheric historic estate, famous for its unusual dual architectural style the house is built with one side in Gothic style and the other in Classical style. Surrounded by sweeping lawns, woodland, and lakes, the estate offers a striking combination of history, architecture, and natural beauty."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The grounds feature formal gardens, walking trails, and a small harbour on Strangford Lough, making it a favourite spot for walkers, photographers, and families. Castle Ward also hosts events and outdoor activities, from archery to horse riding, bringing its historic charm alive for visitors of all ages."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The estate’s rivers, lakes, and forests provide peaceful spots for reflection, wildlife watching, and photography, while the house itself gives insight into 18th-century life. Castle Ward’s blend of architecture, history, and landscape makes it one of County Down’s most photogenic and memorable destinations."}]}]}

title: Vibrant Local Cafe & Eatery
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Right in the heart of Banbridge, you'll find this bustling, modern cafe, a real favourite among those in the know. It's the kind of place you can pop into for a locally roasted coffee and a freshly baked treat, or settle in for a more substantial meal like brunch or a casual dinner. The atmosphere is always lively and welcoming, with big windows perfect for watching the world go by."}]}]}

title: Mediterranean Dining in a Historic Mill
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set inside a beautifully restored 18th-century watermill, this spot is a true gem just outside Belfast. You'll find a stunning, light-filled dining room upstairs with gorgeous views, serving vibrant, Mediterranean-inspired dishes with a strong Italian heart. The atmosphere is elegant yet relaxed, making it perfect for a special lunch or dinner, and don't miss the fantastic deli downstairs to take a taste of your experience home with you."}]}]}

title: Coastal Lighthouse Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"At the southern tip of the Lecale peninsula, near the village of Killough, St John’s Lighthouse stands as a striking sentinel overlooking the Irish Sea and the entrance to Dundrum Bay. Its bold black tower with yellow bands is instantly recognisable against the rugged coastline, and its powerful light has guided ships safely along this once treacherous stretch of water since the mid-1800s."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Rising to around 40 metres above the rocky shore, it is the tallest onshore lighthouse in Ireland. Originally built in 1844 and later raised to its full height, the lighthouse played a vital role in maritime safety, warning vessels of the rocky coast and guiding them into nearby harbours."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Today, while the tower itself is usually closed to the public, the surrounding headland and old keepers’ cottages create a wonderfully atmospheric setting. A short coastal walk brings visitors close to the lighthouse’s base, where the sound of surf against rock and wide sea views make it a favourite spot for photographers, walkers, and nature lovers."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"St John’s Lighthouse combines maritime history with wild, beautiful scenery, standing as both a beacon of the past and a highlight of the County Down coast."}]}]}

title: Enchanting Woodland Tipi Cafe
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away in the enchanting Finnebrogue Woods, you'll find this magical tipi café and farm shop, a true local secret. It's the perfect spot to unwind by a log fire with some of the best coffee and cake around after a gentle stroll on the woodland trails. They're famous for their own Dexter beef burgers, which are a must-try, and the whole place has a wonderfully relaxed, dog-friendly vibe that makes you want to linger all afternoon."}]}]}

title: Seaside Vegetarian Cafe
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Birch Newcastle is a popular independent coffee shop located on Central Promenade in Newcastle, County Down. Known for its relaxed, modern vibe and high-quality coffee, it’s a favourite spot for both locals and visitors. The café offers a great selection of brunch dishes, baked goods, and vegetarian-friendly options, with a focus on fresh, well-sourced ingredients. With its friendly atmosphere, seaside location, and consistently excellent coffee, Birch is a go-to stop for a laid-back breakfast or catch-up by the coast."}]}]}

title: Artisan Bakery & Coffee
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"If you're in Newcastle, you absolutely have to pop into Kin & Folk for what might just be the best sandwich of your life. The focaccia is unbelievably soft, and the fillings are out of this world, all complemented by some of the finest coffee around. It’s a cozy spot, perfect for grabbing a heavenly pastry or a delicious carrot muffin to brighten your day."}]}]}

title: Historic Pub & Dining
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"One of Hillsborough's oldest pubs, a charming and rustic spot that's been welcoming locals since 1752. You'll find a cozy atmosphere with roaring open fires in the winter and a lovely cobblestone beer garden for sunny days. The menu champions local produce, offering hearty, upscale pub fare that perfectly complements their wide selection of ales, beers, and whiskeys."}]}]}

title: Hike the North of Ireland’s Highest Peak
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A proper Mourne Mountains challenge with a rewarding summit. The trek up Slieve Donard, The North of Ireland's highest peak, begins at the Donard car park and leads you through a lovely forest path alongside the Glen River. Once you clear the trees, the path continues to the saddle between Slieve Commedagh and Slieve Donard before the real work begins! The final, steep ascent follows the iconic Mourne Wall right to the top. The round trip is about 9km and can take four to five hours, but the panoramic views of the coast and surrounding peaks are absolutely worth the effort."}]}]}

title: The Big Stone with Big Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For some of the best views around, you'll want to head up to the Cloughmore Stone, or 'The Big Stone' as we call it. You can drive most of the way up through the lovely Kilbroney Park to the upper car park, and from there it's a short but steep 10-minute hike to the massive granite boulder. Once you're there, the panoramic scenery overlooking Carlingford Lough and the Mournes is absolutely breathtaking, making that little climb completely worth it."}]}]}

title: Beach Walk with Mountain Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just outside Newcastle, at the foot of the Mourne Mountains, Murlough Beach is one of the most beautiful and unspoilt beaches in Northern Ireland. Stretching for miles along Dundrum Bay, this wide sweep of golden sand is backed by rolling dunes and open grassland, creating a dramatic and natural coastal landscape."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Murlough forms part of a National Nature Reserve, home to rare plants, birdlife, and delicate dune systems. A network of wooden boardwalks leads visitors across the dunes and down to the shore, helping to protect the fragile environment while offering stunning views of the sea and mountains beyond."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The beach is ideal for long walks, picnics, and photography, especially on clear days when the Mournes rise sharply behind the shoreline. The sound of waves, open skies, and wide horizons give Murlough a peaceful, wild atmosphere that feels far removed from nearby towns."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Popular with walkers, families, and nature lovers, Murlough Beach is a perfect place to experience the raw beauty of County Down’s coastline and one of the finest sandy beaches in the region."}]}]}

title: Enchanting Riverside Walk
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Take a wander down to the Fairy Glen, a truly enchanting spot right at the edge of the village. You'll follow a lovely, easy-going path alongside the Kilbroney River, with the sound of the water creating a peaceful soundtrack for your stroll. It's a gorgeous walk in any season, connecting you directly with the wider trails of Kilbroney Park. The main circular trail is about 3.7km and should take you roughly an hour to complete."}]}]}

title: Charming Seaside Swimming Cove
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away along the coastal path, Brompton Bay is a charming little cove perfect for a refreshing dip or simply relaxing by the water. It's a favourite spot for local sea swimmers, with steps and a handrail offering easy access into the sheltered water. You can also join the scenic North Down Coastal Path here for a longer walk with lovely views. Parking is on the roadside, so you'll have a very short walk of a minute or two down to the bay itself."}]}]}

title: A Sweeping Sandy Escape
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A magnificent two-kilometre stretch of flat, golden sand with breathtaking views of the Mourne Mountains in the distance, Tyrella Beach is a favourite with locals and visitors alike. It’s an ideal spot for a refreshing walk, a family day out, or simply enjoying the fresh sea air and open space."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The beach is a car-free zone, helping to preserve its peaceful atmosphere. Access is via a short walk from the car park, leading through protected sand dunes within a conservation area before opening out onto the wide, expansive shoreline."}]}]}

title: Enchanting Forest River Walk
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tollymore Forest Park is a beautiful and historic park located near Bryansford in County Down, Northern Ireland, at the foothills of the Mourne Mountains. Covering over 600 hectares, it is known for its dramatic scenery, including rivers, bridges, waterfalls, and dense woodlands. The park features a network of walking trails ranging from easy riverside strolls to more challenging hikes into the Mournes, making it ideal for walkers, runners, and nature lovers. Tollymore is also famous for its forests of tall conifers and broadleaf trees, some of which were planted in the 18th century, and its well-preserved medieval-style bridges and gates, adding historical charm to the natural beauty."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Tollymore Forest Park is a popular spot for photography and wildlife watching, and it served as a filming location for scenes in Game of "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"link","attrs":{"href":"http://Thrones.so","target":"_blank","rel":"noopener noreferrer nofollow","class":"text-title underline"}}],"text":"Thrones."},{"type":"text","text":" So it can be busy in summer!"}]}]}

title: A Mountain & Lake Adventure
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Castlewellan Forest Park is a stunning 450-hectare park located just outside the town of Castlewellan in County Down, Northern Ireland. It combines natural beauty with historic landscaping, featuring a large lake, rolling woodlands, walking and cycling trails, and areas for horse riding. The park is home to a nationally important arboretum, planted in the early 20th century, which showcases trees and plants from around the world. A highlight is the Castlewellan Peace Maze, one of the largest permanent hedge mazes in the UK and Ireland, symbolizing peace and reconciliation. With its mix of outdoor activities, scenic spots, and family-friendly attractions, the park is a perfect place for nature lovers, walkers, and anyone looking to enjoy the Mourne countryside."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: The park is especially beautiful in spring when the flowers bloom and in autumn when the trees display vibrant colors."}]}]}

title: Luxury Lakeside Cabin Retreat
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Star rating: 5-star luxury nature retreat"}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"What makes it unique: Secluded, sustainable cabins set on a private 200-acre estate in County Down, offering the perfect blend of modern comfort and immersive natural surroundings."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Birch Cabins provides a luxurious escape in the heart of County Down’s wild landscapes, allowing guests to disconnect from daily life and reconnect with nature. The property combines contemporary, eco-friendly design with warm, inviting interiors, creating a serene atmosphere amidst forests, lakes, and rolling countryside."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes beautifully designed cabins, each thoughtfully crafted with modern amenities, comfortable furnishings, and private outdoor spaces. Guests can relax in their own wood-burning hot tub on a private balcony, or simply enjoy the peaceful surroundings from the comfort of their cabin."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Birch Cabins emphasizes local and seasonal ingredients, perfect for preparing meals in the fully equipped kitchens or enjoying simple picnics by the lake. For those who prefer to explore, nearby towns and scenic trails provide options for fresh local cuisine and outdoor adventure."}]},{"type":"paragraph"},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its secluded location, elegant cabin design, and connection to nature, Birch Cabins is ideal for couples, small groups, or anyone seeking a stylish, tranquil retreat away from the bustle of city life."}]}]}

title: Historic Castle and Museums
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step back in time at Enniskillen Castle, beautifully situated right on the banks of the River Erne. Inside the historic walls, you'll find two fascinating museums that bring the area's rich local and military history to life. It's a perfect way to spend an afternoon, with self-guided tours allowing you to explore the ancient stronghold and its exhibits at your own pace."}]}]}

title: Stately Home & Parkland Walk
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set within a vast landscaped estate just outside Enniskillen, Castle Coole is one of the finest examples of neoclassical architecture in Ireland. Managed by the National Trust, the mansion stands at the centre of a sweeping parkland of mature trees, open lawns, and quiet woodland paths. Its symmetrical façade and restrained elegance reflect the grandeur of the late eighteenth century."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Designed by architect James Wyatt, Castle Coole was built for the Earls of Belmore and is renowned for its refined interiors. Visitors can explore richly furnished rooms, intricate plasterwork, and a grand staircase that highlights the craftsmanship of the period. The house also features extensive basement servants’ quarters, offering insight into the scale of estate life behind the scenes."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Beyond the mansion, the estate provides ample space for walking and cycling. Waymarked trails wind through woodland and along the shores of Lough Coole, creating a peaceful setting that contrasts with the formality of the house itself. The grounds are popular for relaxed strolls as well as longer outdoor outings."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Seasonal events and guided tours add further depth to a visit, while the stable yard includes a café and visitor facilities. Parking is available on site, and both the house and grounds operate with set opening times, so checking ahead is recommended."}]}]}

title: Enniskillen's Culinary Gem
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away on a side street, you'll find a true local favourite where the chef's fine-dining background shines in a wonderfully relaxed setting. The Street Kitchen is the perfect spot for a flavour-packed brunch or lunch, serving up creative dishes that blend local Irish ingredients with global street food flair. Whether you're grabbing their famous boxty or trying one of the inventive daily specials, it’s a culinary experience that feels both special and comfortably down-to-earth."}]}]}

title: Erne Adventures
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get ready for a unique perspective on Enniskillen, Ireland's only island town, with a fun-filled outing at Erne Adventures. From your starting point on the Sligo Road, you can trade the pavement for the Lough Erne waterways and explore the scenery on a hydrobike or an electric e-board. It’s a fantastic way to see the historic town and its surroundings from the water, offering a memorable experience for families and solo adventurers alike."}]}]}

title: Cosy Lakeside Dining
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"This might be my favourite restaurant in Ireland. Tucked away in the serene woodlands on the edge of Lough Erne, Lasair offers an intimate and cosy dining escape. You'll be welcomed by the warmth of a glowing fireplace into a space that feels both rustic and refined. The menu is a creative celebration of Irish ingredients, focusing on small, shareable plates with bold, playful flavours, making for a truly memorable meal in the heart of the Fermanagh lakelands. "}]}]}

title: Secluded Forest and Lake Escape
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star Rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What Makes It Unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A design-led woodland and lakeside retreat featuring immersive bubble domes, contemporary lodges, and a secluded spa sanctuary in the heart of the Fermanagh Lakelands."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Overview:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Finn Lough is a tranquil, adults-focused retreat set within a private forest on the shores of Lower Lough Erne. Blending nature with minimalist Scandinavian-inspired design, it offers guests the opportunity to disconnect from daily life and reconnect with the surrounding landscape. The atmosphere is peaceful, modern, and intentionally understated."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Accommodation:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"The resort is best known for its luxury Bubble Domes,  transparent, climate-controlled domes that allow guests to sleep beneath the stars while enjoying complete comfort and privacy. Stylish forest lodges and lakeside villas offer additional space, sleek interiors, and floor-to-ceiling views of the woodland or water."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Dining:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Dining focuses on locally sourced, seasonal produce with a relaxed yet refined approach. Intimate dining spaces and curated menus enhance the sense of exclusivity and calm."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Spa & Experience:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"The Elements Trail Spa is a signature feature, a guided thermal spa journey through outdoor hot tubs, saunas, steam rooms, and relaxation spaces woven through the forest. Guests can also enjoy lakeside walks, kayaking, and quiet moments immersed in nature."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ideal For:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Couples seeking a romantic, design-focused escape, wellness enthusiasts, and travellers looking for a unique, nature-immersed luxury experience in Ireland."}]}]}

title: Luxury Lakeside Escape
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star Rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 5-star luxury"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What Makes It Unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A world-class lakeside resort set on a 600-acre peninsula between two loughs, home to championship golf courses and an award-winning Thai-inspired spa."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Overview:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Lough Erne Resort is a prestigious five-star destination near Enniskillen, County Fermanagh, surrounded by the tranquil waters of Lower Lough Erne and Castle Hume Lough. Its dramatic setting on a private peninsula offers panoramic lake views, manicured grounds, and a sense of secluded grandeur. The resort seamlessly blends traditional country-house elegance with contemporary luxury."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Accommodation:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Rooms and suites are spacious and refined, featuring plush furnishings, marble bathrooms, and many with private balconies overlooking the lakes. Luxury lodges and suites provide additional privacy and space, ideal for extended stays or special occasions."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Dining:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"The resort offers a choice of elegant dining experiences, showcasing locally sourced Northern Irish produce. Fine dining restaurants, stylish bars, and relaxed lounges provide a range of atmospheres, from celebratory meals to casual lakeside drinks."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Golf & Wellness:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Home to two championship golf courses, including the renowned Faldo Course, the resort is a leading golf destination in Ireland. The Thai-inspired spa is a highlight, offering holistic treatments, thermal suites, and serene relaxation spaces overlooking the water."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Experience:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Guests can enjoy boating, walking trails, cycling, and exploring the scenic Fermanagh Lakelands. With its combination of world-class golf, luxury spa facilities, and breathtaking lakeside setting, Lough Erne Resort delivers an indulgent and memorable escape."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ideal For:"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":"Golf enthusiasts, spa lovers, couples seeking a romantic retreat, and travellers looking for five-star lakeside luxury in Northern Ireland."}]}]}

title: A Scenic Fermanagh Hike
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"One of the most recognised hikes in Ireland, the Cuilcagh Boardwalk Trail leads walkers across a wide expanse of blanket bog in a protected upland landscape. Located within Cuilcagh Lakelands Geopark, the route begins on a gravel track before transitioning onto an elevated wooden boardwalk designed to protect the fragile peatland beneath. The open terrain and sweeping views give the walk a strong sense of space from the outset."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"As the trail progresses, the boardwalk steadily climbs toward the upper slopes of Cuilcagh Mountain. The final section features a steep series of steps, often referred to as the Stairway to Heaven, which ascends sharply to a viewing platform near the summit. From here, expansive views stretch across counties Fermanagh and Cavan, rewarding the effort of the climb."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The full return distance is approximately eleven to twelve kilometres, and most walkers should allow several hours to complete the route. While the path is clearly marked and well maintained, the steady incline and exposed conditions make it a moderately challenging outing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Weather can change quickly at higher elevation, and strong winds are common near the summit. Suitable footwear, layered clothing, and adequate water are essential for a safe and comfortable hike. Parking is available at the designated trailhead, though advance booking may be required during busy periods."}]}]}

title: Underground River Adventure
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Prepare for an incredible journey beneath the rolling green hills of Fermanagh as you explore one of Europe's most spectacular show caves. Your adventure begins with a magical boat ride on the subterranean river, water levels permitting, gliding through vast, echoing chambers. You'll then follow your guide on foot along winding passages, marveling at the stunning rock formations that have been sculpted over millions of years. Be ready for a bit of a climb on your way out, with about 150 steps leading you back to the surface, but the otherworldly sights are more than worth the effort."}]}]}

title: Refreshing Dip in Blacksod Bay
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Located along the Atlantic edge of the Mullet Peninsula, the Béal an Mhuirthead Tidal Pool offers a safe and scenic place to swim in natural seawater. Set into the rocky shoreline near Belmullet, the pool fills with the tide, creating a sheltered area that allows for sea swimming without the full force of the open ocean. The surrounding coastline remains rugged and exposed, adding to the sense of place."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The pool is particularly popular with local swimmers and visitors during the warmer months. Its defined stone walls help retain water at lower tide levels, making it accessible for much of the day. Clear Atlantic water and uninterrupted sea views give the experience a distinctly coastal character."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Swimming conditions vary depending on tide and weather. Calm days provide a more relaxed setting, while breezy conditions remind visitors of the power of the nearby ocean. As with all sea swimming locations, water temperatures remain cool even in summer."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"There are limited facilities on site, and parking is available nearby. Visitors should check tide times before arrival and exercise caution on wet or uneven rocks around the pool area."}]}]}

title: Wild Atlantic Sea Stack
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perched dramatically on the Atlantic coast of County Mayo, Downpatrick Head commands attention as one of Ireland’s most spectacular and rugged sea cliffs. Towering columns of basalt and sea stacks rise sharply from the crashing waves below, creating a dramatic scene that feels both wild and timeless. From the clifftop paths, the endless horizon of ocean stretches far to the west, giving visitors a sense of exposure and scale unlike anywhere else on the north coast."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The headland is steeped in history and legend. It takes its name from Saint Patrick, who is said to have visited the site, and local folklore adds stories of ancient heroes and dramatic events. The contrast between the raw natural forces of the Atlantic and the layers of human history makes the setting especially memorable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Walking along the cliff trails, visitors encounter spectacular viewpoints at every turn. The famous sea stack, Dun Briste, rises dramatically from the ocean several hundred meters offshore, providing a breathtaking focus for photographs and contemplation. The paths are well maintained, allowing for safe exploration while preserving the wild character of the landscape."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can dramatically change the experience. Stormy days bring crashing waves and wind swept spray, adding to the drama of the cliffs, while calm weather allows for long, peaceful views across the bay. Early mornings and late afternoons are particularly rewarding for photography, when light and shadow accentuate the rugged coastline."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: The cliffs are steep and exposed, so care is essential at all times. Parking is available at the visitor area, with clearly marked paths leading to the main viewpoints. Visitors are advised to stay on designated trails and supervise children closely."}]}]}

title: Ancient Fields on Atlantic Cliffs
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Perched high on the dramatic cliffs of North Mayo, the Céide Fields reveal one of the most significant prehistoric landscapes in the world. Beneath the blanket bog lies an extensive network of stone walled fields, preserved for over five thousand years and offering remarkable insight into early farming life. The exposed coastal setting, with sweeping Atlantic views, adds scale and atmosphere to this extraordinary archaeological site."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Discovered in the twentieth century, the Céide Fields represent the remains of a Neolithic farming community whose carefully planned field systems, dwellings, and tombs were gradually covered by peat over centuries. Today, guided tours lead visitors across boardwalks that protect the fragile landscape while explaining how archaeologists uncovered this hidden past."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The visitor centre enhances the experience with interactive exhibits and panoramic windows overlooking the cliffs. Inside, displays explore ancient farming techniques, daily life, and the environmental changes that shaped the region. Outside, marked trails guide visitors toward viewpoints where land meets ocean in dramatic fashion."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Weather and light greatly influence the visit. Clear days open up vast views across the Atlantic, while mist and wind create a powerful sense of isolation and timelessness. The exposed clifftop position means conditions can change quickly, adding to the atmosphere of the site."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Parking and full visitor facilities are available at the centre, with seasonal opening hours in place. Sturdy footwear and weather appropriate clothing are recommended, particularly when joining guided walks across the bogland and cliff paths."}]}]}

title: Dramatic Cliffside Hiking Adventure
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For some of the most breathtaking and wild coastal scenery in Ireland, you have to experience the Benwee Head loop. Starting from the small Gaeltacht village of Carrowteigue, this challenging 12km hike takes you along the top of staggering cliffs that drop dramatically into the Atlantic, they're even higher than the Cliffs of Moher! You'll be trekking across open bogland and sheep paths, so good boots are a must, but the reward is unparalleled views of rugged sea stacks and the vast Broadhaven Bay. The trail is well-marked but completely exposed to the elements, so be prepared for a true adventure that takes about 3 to 5 hours."}]}]}

title: A Poignant & Scenic Drive
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Cutting through one of the most remote landscapes in County Mayo, the Doolough Valley offers a drive that is as powerful as it is beautiful. The narrow road winds between the dark waters of Doo Lough and Glencullin Lough, with the Sheeffry Hills rising steeply on one side and the Mweelrea mountains dominating the other. The scenery feels vast and untouched, shaped by rock, water, and shifting Atlantic light."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Beyond its striking appearance, the valley carries a profound historical weight. A simple stone cross stands quietly by the roadside, marking the site of the tragic famine walk of 1849, when local people perished while seeking relief. The memorial is understated yet deeply moving, adding a layer of reflection to an already dramatic setting."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The experience here is shaped by stillness and scale. There are no large visitor centres or busy attractions, only open landscape and the sound of wind across the hills. Many visitors pause at the memorial viewpoint, stepping out to absorb both the scenery and the history that defines this place."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Weather plays a significant role in the atmosphere. Clear conditions reveal the full sweep of the valley and its lakes, while mist and rain can lend a haunting, almost solemn character to the mountains. Each visit feels different, influenced by light and season."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Parking areas are located beside the road near the memorial, making it an accessible stop along the route. Care should be taken when pulling in and out, as the road can be narrow and winding in places."}]}]}

title: Historic Abbey Ruins
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get ready for a special experience at Moyne Abbey, one of the most impressive and well-preserved monastic ruins you'll find anywhere in Ireland. To reach this tranquil spot, you'll park carefully on the roadside and take a short walk across a private field, which adds to the feeling of discovering a hidden gem. Once there, you can lose yourself for an hour or two exploring the extensive 15th-century Gothic ruins, from the towering church to the beautifully intact cloisters, all set against the stunning backdrop of Killala Bay."}]}]}

title: Contemporary Art on the Coast
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away in the village of Ballycastle, you'll find a modern gem of a museum showcasing art inspired by the wild North Mayo coast. The collection features works from both Irish and international artists who have spent time here, capturing the unique light and landscape of the area. Take your time exploring the two floors of gallery space, and don't miss the second-floor lounge, where a glass atrium offers breathtaking views over the sea and surrounding hills—it's the perfect spot to sit and reflect."}]}]}

title: Riverside Luxury and Relaxation
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star Rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star boutique luxury"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What Makes It Unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A striking, design-led hotel set along the banks of the River Moy, blending industrial heritage with contemporary style and offering a glass-fronted spa overlooking the water."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Overview:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" The Ice House Hotel is a chic riverside retreat in Ballina, County Mayo, housed in a beautifully restored 19th-century ice storage building. Its bold, modern architecture contrasts dramatically with the rugged beauty of the surrounding landscape, creating a stylish yet tranquil escape along the Wild Atlantic Way."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Accommodation:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Rooms and suites are sleek and contemporary, featuring floor-to-ceiling windows, minimalist décor, and luxurious furnishings. Many offer uninterrupted river views, allowing guests to relax while overlooking the flowing waters of the River Moy. Spacious suites provide added indulgence with freestanding baths and private balconies."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Dining:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" The hotel’s restaurant celebrates the best of local Mayo produce, with seasonal menus that highlight fresh seafood and regional ingredients. The riverside setting enhances the dining experience, creating an atmosphere that is both intimate and scenic."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Wellness & Experience:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" A standout feature is the award-winning spa, perched dramatically over the river. Guests can unwind in outdoor hot tubs, thermal suites, and treatment rooms with panoramic water views. The peaceful riverside setting, combined with modern luxury, makes the Ice House Hotel a rejuvenating escape in the west of Ireland."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ideal For:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Couples seeking a romantic riverside retreat, spa lovers, and travellers exploring the Wild Atlantic Way who appreciate contemporary design paired with natural beauty."}]}]}

title: Climb Ireland's Holy Mountain
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Rising above the landscape of County Mayo, Croagh Patrick is one of Ireland’s most significant and recognisable mountains. Known locally as The Reek, it has long been a place of pilgrimage and reflection, drawing walkers from across the country and beyond. From its slopes, wide views open out across Clew Bay and its many small islands, with the Atlantic stretching beyond."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The mountain holds deep religious and cultural importance, traditionally associated with Saint Patrick, who is said to have fasted on its summit. Today, a small chapel stands at the top, marking the focal point of the climb and reinforcing its spiritual heritage. The combination of sacred tradition and dramatic scenery gives the ascent a unique character."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The standard route begins at the visitor centre car park in Murrisk and covers approximately seven kilometres return.
