Most walkers allow between three and a half to five hours to complete the climb. The lower section follows a steady incline, but the upper slopes become considerably steeper, with loose stones and uneven ground requiring care and good footing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Clear days reward climbers with panoramic views across Mayo, while mist and cloud can create a more atmospheric and contemplative experience. Conditions can change quickly, and strong winds are common near the summit."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Good footwear, layered clothing, and adequate water are essential. The trail can be challenging, particularly on the final ascent, so preparation and awareness of weather conditions are important for a safe and enjoyable climb."}]}]}

title: Achill Island's Hidden Gem
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away on the western edge of Achill Island, Keem Beach is one of Ireland’s most spectacular coastal destinations. The approach itself is unforgettable, with a winding drive that threads through green hills and cliffs before opening onto the horseshoe bay. From the moment you arrive, the golden sands and turquoise waters create a striking contrast with the surrounding landscape, offering a sense of calm and natural beauty that feels almost untouched."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The beach is ideal for both relaxation and adventure. Summer visitors often enjoy a refreshing swim in the clear water, while families and picnickers can take advantage of the wide crescent of sand. For those seeking a higher vantage point, a moderate trail leads up to the old coastguard station, where panoramic views reveal the full sweep of the bay and the rolling hills beyond."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Keem Beach is as much about the journey as the destination. The combination of dramatic cliffs, tranquil waters, and vibrant greenery makes every step along the shore or hillside feel immersive. Photographers, walkers, and nature lovers often linger here, drawn by the sense of space and the ever-changing light across the bay."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can enhance the visit. Calm, sunny days highlight the vivid colours of the sand and sea, while early mornings or late afternoons provide softer light and a quieter experience. Changing tides and weather add constant variety, ensuring that no two visits feel exactly the same."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Parking is available near the beach, and the coastal trail requires sturdy footwear. Visitors are encouraged to follow marked paths and respect the fragile dune environment while exploring the area."}]}]}

title: Wild Atlantic Sea Adventure
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Hop on a boat from Blacksod Pier and let the sea spray awaken your senses as you journey to the deserted Inishkea Islands. You'll get to explore the hauntingly beautiful abandoned village, walk across pristine white sandy beaches, and swim in stunningly clear turquoise waters. Keep your eyes peeled for the locals – playful dolphins, curious seals, and a whole host of seabirds often make an appearance on this unforgettable trip into the heart of the wild Atlantic."}]}]}

title: Historic Woollen Mill & Cafe
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step into a piece of living history at FOXFORD Woollen Mills, where the rhythmic sounds of the looms have been the heartbeat of this town for over a century. You can feel the tradition and craftsmanship in the air as you browse the beautiful woollen throws, scarves, and blankets, all woven right here on site. After exploring the mill and perhaps catching a glimpse of the weavers at work, treat yourself to a bite at the lovely upstairs café, known for its delicious dishes made with fresh, locally sourced ingredients. It’s a wonderfully authentic experience that combines shopping, history, and the best of local flavours."}]}]}

title: Historic Seaside Village
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nestled on the north Mayo coastline, Killala Village commands attention as a charming and historic seaside settlement. With its colourful streets, traditional pubs, and quiet harbour, the village offers a relaxed and welcoming atmosphere. From the main square, the gentle hum of daily life mixes with views across Killala Bay, giving visitors a sense of connection to both community and coastline."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"History is woven into every corner of Killala. The village played a key role in the 1798 Rebellion, and landmarks such as the old cathedral and local heritage sites tell the story of centuries of maritime trade, agriculture, and cultural life. The contrast between its small size and its rich historical tapestry makes the experience especially engaging."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Exploring Killala is easy and rewarding. Strolling along the harbour, wandering the narrow streets, or visiting local shops and cafés allows visitors to absorb the village at their own pace. Families, walkers, and history enthusiasts often linger, drawn by the friendly atmosphere and the mix of scenic and cultural sights rather than any formal tour."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the visit. Mornings bring a peaceful start as fishermen head out across the bay, while afternoons and evenings fill the streets with a gentle local buzz. Seasonal events, such as village festivals and coastal activities, add additional charm throughout the year."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Parking is available in and around the village, and many attractions are within easy walking distance. Comfortable footwear is recommended for exploring the historic streets and nearby coastal paths."}]}]}

title: Explore Wild Mayo Like a Local
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"If you want to experience the real magic of County Mayo, let Ged and his team at Terra Firma be your guides. They create incredible, personalized adventures that get you away from the crowds and deep into the stunning landscapes of the Wild Atlantic Way. Whether you're hiking remote hills, stargazing under some of Europe's darkest skies, or listening to local folklore, you'll feel less like a tourist and more like an old friend being shown the hidden gems of this beautiful coastline."}]}]}

title: A Taste of West Ireland
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away above the bustling Market Square, you'll find a true local gem where dining feels both intimate and exciting. The chefs here have a passion for the West of Ireland that you can taste in every bite, crafting contemporary dishes from ingredients sourced from local suppliers or foraged nearby. It’s a cozy, welcoming spot that offers a genuine taste of Mayo's finest produce, making it the perfect place for a memorable evening."}]}]}

title: Convenient Drive-Thru Coffee & Treats
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"When you're exploring Ballina and need a quick and friendly boost, this locally owned drive-thru coffee spot is a real gem. You can grab a fantastic coffee and delicious, freshly-made pastries like sausage rolls and donuts without ever leaving your car. For those on foot, there's also a convenient walk-up window, making it a perfect pitstop for everyone."}]}]}

title: Ashford Lodge
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star Rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What Makes It Unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A stylish lakeside lodge set within the historic Ashford Estate, offering contemporary elegance and access to one of Ireland’s most iconic castle experiences."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Overview:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Ashford Castle Lodge provides a refined yet relaxed stay on the magnificent Ashford Estate in Cong, County Mayo. Overlooking Lough Corrib and surrounded by expansive parklands and woodland, the lodge combines modern comfort with the heritage and grandeur of its renowned sister property, Ashford Castle. Guests enjoy the tranquillity of a boutique-style retreat while having access to the estate’s exceptional facilities and activities."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Accommodation:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Rooms and suites are bright and contemporary, featuring elegant furnishings, plush bedding, and spacious bathrooms. Many offer views of Lough Corrib or the estate grounds. The design is sophisticated yet welcoming, ideal for guests seeking understated luxury."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Dining:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Guests can dine within the lodge or enjoy a variety of culinary experiences across the wider estate, from relaxed brasserie-style dining to fine dining in the castle itself. Seasonal menus highlight the best of Irish ingredients."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Experience:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":"Stays at Ashford Castle Lodge include access to estate activities such as falconry, boating on Lough Corrib, golf, fishing, and woodland walks. The combination of lakeside serenity and estate grandeur creates a unique and memorable Irish escape."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ideal For:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Couples, families, and travellers looking for a luxury lakeside retreat with access to one of Ireland’s most celebrated estates."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"hardBreak"}]}]}

title: Hike to Ireland's Highest Cliffs
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Prepare for an unforgettable adventure to the summit of Croaghaun, home to Ireland's highest and most dramatic sea cliffs. This is a proper hill walk, not a casual stroll, with no marked trails to guide you. Starting from the beautiful Keem Beach, you'll navigate steep, boggy, and rugged terrain on a challenging 4-6 hour trek to the top. The effort is immense, but the reward is a breathtaking, panoramic view of the wild Atlantic that few ever get to witness. Be sure to wear sturdy, waterproof hiking boots and check the weather, as it can change in an instant out here."}]}]}

title: Coastal Trail Through Ancient Bog
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set along the rugged coastline of County Mayo, the Claggan Mountain Coastal Trail offers a short and accessible walk through a distinctive Atlantic landscape. This two kilometre route combines mountain views, blanket bog, and shoreline scenery, creating a varied experience within a relatively small area. With Achill Island visible in the distance, the setting feels open and expansive despite the gentle scale of the walk."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The trail begins on a well constructed wooden boardwalk that crosses an area of protected blanket bog. This section is level and easy to follow, making it suitable for most visitors. As the route continues, it leads toward a stony beach where ancient pine stumps emerge from the peat, evidence of a forest that once covered the area thousands of years ago."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The full loop, including time spent along the shoreline, typically takes between forty five minutes and one hour to complete. The combination of boardwalk and natural terrain makes it an ideal stop for those looking to stretch their legs without committing to a longer hike. Interpretive signage along the way provides context about the landscape and its ecological significance."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Light and weather play an important role in shaping the experience. Clear conditions highlight the surrounding mountains and coastline, while changing skies add atmosphere to the open bogland. The exposed setting means breezy conditions are common."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Parking is available near the trailhead, with direct access onto the boardwalk. Suitable footwear is recommended for the beach section, particularly after rain, and visitors are encouraged to remain on marked paths to protect the fragile bog environment."}]}]}

title: Explore the Heart of Erris
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Right at the heart of the Mullet Peninsula, you'll find SOLAS, a wonderful new visitor centre that really brings our local stories to life. It’s a place where you can get a true feel for the history and heritage of Erris, from the crucial D-Day weather reports sent from our own Blacksod Lighthouse to tales of the historic offshore islands. The interactive exhibits are fantastic, and after you've spent an hour or so exploring, you can relax in the lovely café with some delicious homemade food."}]}]}

title: Wild Atlantic Blowhole
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Come feel the raw power of the Atlantic at Dún na mBó, one of the wildest spots along our coastline. You'll find a unique sculpture built around a natural blowhole on the clifftop; on a day with a good swell, you can watch the sea spray shoot spectacularly into the air. It’s a short walk from the roadside parking over uneven ground, but the dramatic views of the cliffs and Eagle Island are more than worth it. Just be sure to watch your footing, as the winds here can be quite strong!"}]}]}

title: Ireland's Rural Heritage Museum
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step into the world of old Ireland at this wonderful museum, set in a gorgeous Victorian Gothic house surrounded by beautiful gardens and woodlands. You'll get a real feel for how Irish people lived between 1850 and 1950, with fascinating exhibits spread across four floors that bring the traditions and daily life of the countryside to life. It's a perfect spot to spend a few hours, whether you're exploring the galleries or just enjoying a peaceful walk around the grounds by the lake."}]}]}

title: Wild Nephin National Park
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Ballycroy Visitor Centre serves as a gateway to the untamed landscapes of Wild Nephin National Park. It features an easy 2-kilometre boardwalk trail with panoramic views, interactive exhibits on local nature and history, and the Ginger & Wild Café offering locally-made treats."}]}]}

title: Drogheda's Medieval Gateway
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step back in time at Laurence's Gate, an imposing 13th-century barbican that stands as a proud reminder of Drogheda's medieval past. As the last remaining of the town's original gates, its two grand towers are a truly impressive sight and one of the finest examples of a fortified gateway in Europe. While you can no longer climb to the top for the commanding views of the River Boyne, just standing beneath its archway gives you a powerful sense of the history that has passed through it."}]}]}

title: Hike with Lough Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Slieve Foye Loop is a challenging but rewarding 9km trail that takes you up the slopes of the highest peak in the Cooley Mountains. Starting from the village of Carlingford, the trail winds up through forest tracks and onto open mountainside. The initial climb is steep, but the panoramic views of Carlingford Lough and the Mourne Mountains are worth the effort. The trail can be tricky to follow and wet underfoot, so good hiking boots are essential."}]}]}

title: Dundalk's Rustic Farmyard Haven
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just off the motorway near Dundalk, you'll find Strandfield, a real local treasure set in a beautifully converted farmyard. It's more than just a café; it's a whole experience with a florist, a grocer selling organic produce, and a bakery all in one. You can settle into the rustic, cozy atmosphere for some fantastic vegetarian food, including amazing wood-fired pizzas and delicious homemade cakes, all made with fresh, local ingredients."}]}]}

title: Historic Abbey Ruins
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step back in time at Mellifont Abbey, the serene ruins of Ireland's very first Cistercian monastery, nestled peacefully in the Boyne Valley. As you wander through the foundations, you can almost hear the echoes of the past and picture the lives of the monks who once walked these grounds. The real highlight is the stunning 13th-century octagonal lavabo, where they would wash their hands before meals—it’s a truly unique piece of architecture. The site is just a short stroll from the car park, and while the visitor centre has seasonal hours, you can explore the atmospheric grounds for free all year round."}]}]}

title: Creative Coffee and Sandwiches
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away at Kirks Service Station, this is your go-to spot for some of the most creative and satisfying sandwiches in Dundalk. Forget your usual fare; here you'll find unique combinations and specialty drinks that you just won't get anywhere else. It's a small place with a big personality, perfect for grabbing a top-notch coffee and a seriously tasty bite on the go."}]}]}

title: Historic Castle with Stunning Views
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Overlooking the narrow waters of Carlingford Lough, Carlingford Castle stands as a striking reminder of the town’s medieval past. Perched on a rocky outcrop at the edge of the harbour, the castle commands wide views across the lough toward the Cooley Mountains. Its sturdy stone walls and commanding position reflect both its defensive purpose and its importance in controlling this historic coastal route."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Built in the twelfth century, the fortress was designed to protect the area following the Norman invasion of Ireland. Though partially in ruins today, the surviving tower and curtain walls offer a clear sense of its original strength and strategic significance. The contrast between the solid medieval masonry and the colourful village streets below creates a memorable visual scene."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A short walk uphill from the centre of Carlingford leads visitors to the castle grounds. While interior access can vary, the exterior alone provides impressive views of the harbour and surrounding landscape. The setting invites visitors to pause and imagine the centuries of trade, conflict, and daily life that once unfolded beneath its walls."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Light and weather enhance the experience. Clear days reveal sweeping panoramas across the lough, while softer evening light casts warm tones over the stonework. The elevated position makes it an especially rewarding viewpoint at sunset."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"There are no extensive visitor facilities on site, and the ground can be uneven in places. Comfortable footwear is recommended, and visitors should take care along the exposed edges while exploring the area."}]}]}

title: A Modern Irish Welcome
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step into mo chara, which fittingly means \"my friend,\" and you'll instantly feel the warm, modern energy buzzing through this reimagined Irish pub. It's a place born from friendship, designed to create new ones over pints of their own craft beer and delicious, inventive pizzas or toasties. You'll find it's more than just a bar; it's the town's living room, hosting everything from local markets to comedy nights, ensuring there's always a good reason to gather."}]}]}

title: Historic Forest & Lakeside Trails
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just outside Monaghan town, Rossmore Park offers an expansive area of woodland, lakes, and walking trails set within a historic estate landscape with sculptures throughout. Mature trees line the avenues, quiet water reflects the surrounding greenery, and the remains of Rossmore Castle stand partially hidden among the forest, adding a subtle historical presence to the grounds. "}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The park is well laid out, with clearly marked routes that cater to a range of abilities. Short, level paths near the car park suit relaxed walks, while longer woodland loops provide a more substantial outing. All trails begin from the main parking area, making navigation straightforward and accessible."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Wildlife and seasonal colour shape much of the experience. In early summer, the rhododendrons come into bloom, bringing strong bursts of colour to the shaded pathways. Throughout the year, the lakes and wooded areas attract birds and create a calm setting for walking or quiet reflection."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Facilities include car parking, open green spaces, and designated walking routes. Surfaces can be uneven in places, so appropriate footwear is recommended, particularly after wet weather."}]}]}

title: A force of nature
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set within a sheltered valley in County Leitrim, Glencar Waterfall is one of the most charming natural sights in the northwest of Ireland. Flowing gracefully over a rocky ledge and down into a quiet pool below, the waterfall is framed by woodland and steep green slopes that create a peaceful and intimate setting. Though not the tallest in the country, its elegant drop and accessible location make it especially appealing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The waterfall is perhaps best known for its connection to the poet W B Yeats, who referenced it in his poem The Stolen Child. That literary link adds a sense of folklore and imagination to the scene, enhancing the feeling that this is a place where nature and storytelling meet. After heavy rainfall, the cascade becomes more dramatic, with water surging over the rocks in a continuous white ribbon."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Access is straightforward and suitable for most visitors. A short, clearly marked path leads from the car park to a viewing platform at the base of the falls. The gentle walk passes through trees and along the edge of Glencar Lough, offering glimpses of the surrounding landscape along the way."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Seasonal changes bring different moods to the waterfall. In spring and summer, fresh greenery surrounds the cascade, while autumn introduces warm colours to the woodland. Even on overcast days, the soft light and sound of falling water create a calm and reflective atmosphere."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Parking, picnic areas, and basic visitor facilities are available on site. The path may be damp or uneven in places, so comfortable footwear is recommended, particularly after rain."}]}]}

title: Loughside Castle
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Overlooking the calm waters of Lough Gill in County Leitrim, Parkes Castle stands as a striking reminder of Ireland’s turbulent past. Set against a backdrop of wooded hills and reflective shoreline, the restored fortified manor house occupies a peaceful yet historically strategic position near the water’s edge. Its sturdy stone walls and corner towers create a strong first impression, blending defensive design with domestic character."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Built in the early seventeenth century on the site of an earlier Gaelic stronghold, the castle reflects a period of transition in Irish history. Once home to the English planter Sir Roger Parke, it represents the changing political and cultural landscape of the time. Careful restoration has revealed both the fortified outer walls and the more refined interior living quarters, allowing visitors to appreciate its layered history."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Inside the courtyard, timber galleries, stone staircases, and period furnishings recreate the atmosphere of a plantation era residence. Informative displays guide visitors through the story of the original O Rourke clan who held the site before the arrival of the settlers, offering insight into the complex heritage of the region."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The setting itself adds greatly to the experience. Lough Gill stretches quietly beside the castle, its waters reflecting the surrounding countryside and adding a sense of calm to what was once a defensive stronghold. The changing light across the lake enhances the character of the site throughout the day."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visitor facilities and parking are available on site, with seasonal opening hours in place. As access to upper levels involves stairs and uneven surfaces, comfortable footwear is recommended for exploring the grounds and interior spaces."}]}]}

title: Luxury Woodland Sanctuary
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star Rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star luxury"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What Makes It Unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A secluded sanctuary set within 100 acres of private woodland on the banks of the River Shannon, offering luxurious forest lodges, a unique outdoor wellbeing sanctuary, and immersive nature experiences."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Overview:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Drumhierny Woodland Hideaway is a four-star luxury retreat that invites guests to disconnect and recharge in complete tranquillity. Nestled in expansive woodlands, the hideaway combines modern comfort with the natural beauty of County Leitrim. Its setting along the River Shannon, with 5km of woodland trails and hidden fairy forts, makes it a haven for relaxation, exploration, and rejuvenation."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Accommodation:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Lodges are designed to blend seamlessly into the forest, offering stylish interiors, luxurious bedding, and modern amenities. Each lodge provides a private, intimate experience, perfect for couples, families, or solo travellers seeking a serene escape."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Dining and Wellbeing:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Guests can enjoy locally inspired cuisine and casual meals designed to complement the peaceful woodland surroundings. The outdoor wellbeing sanctuary is a standout feature, with hot tubs and traditional seaweed baths nestled within a historic walled garden, creating a uniquely restorative experience."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Experience:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Whether wandering woodland trails, discovering hidden fairy forts, or soaking in the serene wellbeing sanctuary, every moment at Drumhierny Woodland Hideaway is designed to restore mind, body, and soul. Attentive, personalised service ensures a luxurious and memorable stay in harmony with nature."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Ideal For:"},{"type":"hardBreak","marks":[{"type":"bold"}]},{"type":"text","text":" Couples seeking a romantic retreat, wellness enthusiasts, nature lovers, or anyone looking for a luxurious hideaway immersed in Ireland’s woodland beauty."}]}]}

title: Authentic Italian Dining Experience
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For a true taste of Italy right in the heart of Carrick-On-Shannon, you'll want to settle in at Di Vino on Bridge Street. It’s a wonderfully cozy and welcoming spot where the staff, a mix of Italians and locals, make you feel right at home. You can expect classic, authentic dishes made from scratch, from heavenly bruschetta to perfectly crafted pizzas and pastas, all beautifully presented. It's the kind of place perfect for a relaxed meal, whether you're craving a hearty dinner or a delicious dessert like their homemade Tiramisu."}]}]}

title: Award-Winning Riverside Dining
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step into this warm and welcoming gastropub on the banks of the River Shannon, a place that feels like a true local secret. You'll find a cozy, old-world charm inside, complete with polished dark wood and the occasional gentle strum of a live guitar creating a perfectly mellow atmosphere. The food is the real star here, celebrating the best of local Irish ingredients with a modern touch—the seafood, in particular, is always a fantastic choice."}]}]}

title: Lakeside Sauna & Wellness
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nestled on the shores of Lough Allen, this is the perfect spot to unwind and reconnect with nature. You can spend a blissful couple of hours alternating between the heat of the wood-fired saunas and a refreshing dip in the cold plunge pool, all while soaking in the gorgeous mountain views. Whether you're looking for a peaceful yoga session in a yurt or a relaxing time in the hot tub, the welcoming and friendly atmosphere here makes it a real local gem for recharging your batteries."}]}]}

title: Floating Lakeside Boardwalk Stroll
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Take a gentle stroll along Ireland's first floating boardwalk at Acres Lake, a truly unique way to experience the tranquility of the Shannon Blueway. This flat, accessible 600-meter path lets you walk right out over the water, surrounded by stunning scenery and the calm of the lake. It's the perfect spot for a peaceful walk, connecting to a longer 6.5km trail if you're feeling more adventurous, and the main car park is right at the entrance."}]}]}

title: Jinny's Tearooms at Acres
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nestled on the shores of Acres Lake, this charming, family-run tearoom is a beloved local spot for a reason. You can't beat the aroma of freshly baked bread as you walk in, a promise of the wholesome, delicious food to come, much of it made with ingredients grown right on site. It's the perfect place to refuel with a hearty lunch or a sweet treat after a stroll along the beautiful Blueway boardwalk, which is just across the road."}]}]}

title: Sligo's Scenic Surf Haven
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Welcome to Strandhill, a true gem on the Wild Atlantic Way where the energy is simply infectious. You'll find a vast stretch of golden sand pounded by Atlantic waves, with the majestic Knocknarea mountain providing a stunning backdrop. It's a world-renowned spot for surfing, and the village buzzes with a cool, vibrant atmosphere from the surf schools, cozy cafes, and pubs. While the powerful currents make it unsafe for swimming, a long walk along the shore, exploring the dunes, or just watching the surfers ride the swell is an experience in itself. Everything is conveniently located right by the beach, with parking available along the promenade."}]}]}

title: Scenic Atlantic Coast Beach
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Discover one of Sligo's gems, Rosses Point Beach, a beautiful stretch of sand perfect for swimming or strolling. Enjoy breathtaking views of Benbulben and Knocknarea against the Atlantic. It's an ideal spot for families to experience the Wild Atlantic Way, with convenient car park access."}]}]}

title: Sligo's Secret Scenic Drive
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Encircled by towering limestone cliffs in north County Sligo, the Gleniff Horseshoe commands attention as one of Ireland’s most dramatic mountain landscapes. This striking glacial valley forms a natural amphitheatre beneath the imposing slopes of Benbulbin and its neighbouring peaks. Quiet roads and open farmland sit beneath sheer rock faces, giving the area a powerful yet peaceful presence."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many scenic drives, the Gleniff Horseshoe combines geology with folklore and history. The looped route traces the shape of the valley, revealing caves, old mining remains, and stories woven into the surrounding mountains. The contrast between the vast cliffs above and the narrow winding road below makes the journey especially memorable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about atmosphere as it is about movement. Some choose to drive the circular route, while others walk or cycle, allowing more time to absorb the scale of the landscape. Photographers, hikers, and sightseers often pause along the way, drawn by the shifting light and the sense of isolation rather than any formal attraction."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the experience. Clear days highlight the texture and height of the limestone walls, while mist and low cloud add drama and mystery to the valley. Early mornings and quieter evenings offer the most peaceful conditions, with fewer vehicles on the route."}]}]}

title: Knocknarea Cairn
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A memorable climb to Knocknarea Cairn, a massive stone tomb on the summit of Knocknarea mountain. The trail offers breathtaking panoramic views of Sligo Bay, Ben Bulben, and the Atlantic coastline. The cairn is believed to be the final resting place of the legendary warrior, Queen Maeve."}]}]}

title: Wild Atlantic Scenery
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Standing boldly against the Atlantic Ocean, Mullaghmore Head offers one of the most dramatic coastal experiences in County Sligo. This sweeping headland curves out from the charming harbour village of Mullaghmore, where fishing boats rest quietly before the landscape opens into vast sea views and rugged cliffs. The atmosphere here is defined by space, light, and the constant rhythm of the waves."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The scenery is exceptional in every direction. Benbulben rises prominently across the bay, its distinctive flat summit forming a striking backdrop to the coastline. On especially clear days, the cliffs of Donegal are visible across the water, adding depth and scale to an already expansive panorama. The blend of mountain and ocean gives Mullaghmore a character that feels both powerful and serene."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A looped coastal road traces the edge of the headland, making exploration straightforward and rewarding. Visitors can drive slowly around the circuit or enjoy an easy walk from the village, pausing at various points to take in the views. Each curve reveals a new perspective, from sheltered inlets to exposed rock faces where Atlantic swells break dramatically."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Conditions shape the experience. Calm evenings cast warm light over the sea, while windy weather transforms the headland into a spectacle of crashing surf and swirling spray. No matter the season, Mullaghmore Head remains a place defined by its natural energy and open horizons."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Care is advised near cliff edges, particularly during strong winds. Parking is available in the village, with direct access to the loop road and nearby coastal viewpoints."}]}]}

title: Ireland's Tallest Waterfall
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get ready for a truly magical walk to Sruth in Aghaidh an Aird, which you might hear called the Devil's Chimney. It’s home to Ireland's tallest waterfall, and if you time your visit after a good rain, you're in for a treat. The trail itself is a lovely 2.5km loop that takes about an hour, climbing up through peaceful woodland with stunning views over Glencar Lough. On a windy day, you might even see the famous sight of the water blowing straight back up the cliff face. It's a bit of a steep climb in places, but the path is well-kept and the view from the base of the falls is absolutely worth the effort."}]}]}

title: Ireland's Best Burger
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For a burger that's taken seriously, you have to check out Flipside on Rockwood Parade. This isn't your average burger joint; they've earned the title of \"Best Burger in Ireland\" by focusing on incredible, locally sourced ingredients, like grass-fed Angus beef from a Sligo butcher. The atmosphere is always buzzing and vibrant, making it a perfect spot to settle in with friends, grab a craft beer, and tackle a creative masterpiece like \"The Dictator\" buttermilk fried chicken burger."}]}]}

title: Benbulben Summit Trailhead
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Rising dramatically above the landscape of County Sligo, Benbulbin commands attention as one of Ireland’s most distinctive natural landmarks. Its flat topped summit and sheer limestone cliffs dominate the skyline, creating a powerful presence that has inspired artists, writers, and walkers for generations. Visible from miles around, the mountain feels both imposing and inviting, set against a backdrop of rolling countryside and Atlantic light."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many peaks, Benbulbin combines striking geology with deep cultural connections. Formed during the Ice Age, its layered rock face tells a story of ancient natural forces, while local folklore and poetry add further richness to its identity. The contrast between its rugged cliffs and the softer green fields below makes the setting especially memorable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about atmosphere as it is about achievement. The popular Luke’s Bridge route offers a rewarding hike along forest paths and open mountain terrain, gradually revealing sweeping views across Sligo Bay and the surrounding hills. Walkers often pause to take in the silence and scale of the landscape, drawn by the sense of space and perspective rather than simply reaching the summit."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the experience. Clear days provide wide coastal panoramas, while shifting cloud and mist add drama to the cliffs. Early starts are recommended for both quieter trails and safer conditions, as weather on the mountain can change quickly."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: The hike requires good footwear, preparation, and awareness of changing conditions. Parking is available at designated trailheads, and visitors are encouraged to follow marked routes and respect the natural environment."}]}]}

title: Secluded Coastal Haven
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Come and discover the quiet beauty of Portavade, a truly secluded spot on the Sligo coast. It's a peaceful little haven near the Ballysodare Bay inlet, where you can escape the crowds and just breathe in the fresh sea air. You'll be treated to gorgeous views across to Knocknarea, and it's the perfect place for a bit of quiet reflection or exploring the rich sea life in the rock pools."}]}]}

title: Climb to Mythic Caves
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For one of the most rewarding views in Sligo, the hike up to the Keshcorran Caves is well worth the effort. I remember starting from the small car park at the base, knowing it would be short but steep, and feeling the climb in my legs almost straight away. It takes about 20 to 25 minutes to reach the caves, but the steady incline makes it feel like a proper workout."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"As you gain height, the views begin to open up behind you, stretching across the Sligo countryside. Reaching the limestone caves themselves feels like discovering something ancient and slightly hidden. They’re steeped in local folklore and history, and standing at the entrance, looking out over the landscape below, gives a real sense of why people have been drawn to this place for centuries."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I found myself lingering at the top, partly to catch my breath and partly because the panoramic views are genuinely impressive. It’s one of those spots where the effort makes the reward feel even better."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It’s important to take care on the way up and down. The path can be slippery, especially after rain, and sturdy shoes make a real difference. But if you’re prepared for a short, sharp climb, Keshcorran delivers a memorable mix of history, legend, and sweeping scenery."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"hardBreak"}]}]}

title: A Medieval Alleyway
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step back in time as you wander through the Butter Slip, one of our most charming medieval alleyways. This covered stone passageway, built in 1616, connects St. Kieran's Street to the High Street and feels like a secret portal to Kilkenny's past. It gets its name from the butter vendors who once lined the lane on market days, and you can still feel the history in its worn stone steps and atmospheric lanterns."}]}]}

title: Ireland's Fortress Monastery
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just a short drive from Kilkenny, you'll find the incredible ruins of Kells Priory, a place we locals affectionately call the 'Seven Castles.' From the car park, it's a lovely few minutes' walk through a field, usually dotted with sheep, to reach the site. Once inside, you're free to wander through the vast, fortress-like walls and towers, imagining the centuries of history they've witnessed. It’s a remarkably peaceful and sprawling site, perfect for exploring at your own pace."}]}]}

title: Historic Riverside Castle
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"the banks of the River Nore, Kilkenny Castle commands attention as one of Ireland’s most impressive and recognisable historic landmarks. Dominating the skyline of Kilkenny city for over eight centuries, its grand towers and expansive parklands give the setting a sense of strength and elegance. The wide lawns and tree lined avenues surrounding the castle create an inviting approach that highlights both its scale and its beauty."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many medieval fortresses, Kilkenny Castle combines defensive origins with refined later design. Originally built in the twelfth century, it evolved into a stately home, with richly decorated interiors, grand staircases, and an impressive Long Gallery reflecting centuries of change. The contrast between its Norman foundations and its Victorian era splendour makes the visit especially memorable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about atmosphere as it is about history. Exploring the restored state rooms, strolling through the formal gardens, or relaxing in the surrounding parkland allows visitors to experience the castle at their own pace. Families, history enthusiasts, and casual walkers often linger, drawn by the blend of culture and open space in the heart of the city."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the experience. Bright afternoons bring the gardens and riverbank to life, while quieter mornings offer a more peaceful opportunity to admire the architecture and interiors. Seasonal exhibitions and cultural events throughout the year add fresh interest to each visit."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Entry to the interior requires a ticket, while the surrounding parklands are freely accessible. Parking is available nearby in the city, with level pathways around the grounds and some stair access within the historic building."}]}]}

title: A Historic Stroll in Kilkenny
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"As you stroll across John's Bridge, you're treading on a site steeped in centuries of Kilkenny's history, connecting the city over the River Nore since at least the 13th century. The elegant, single-arch bridge you see today was built in 1910 and offers lovely views of the river and the majestic Kilkenny Castle. It's a perfect spot for a photo, but it also holds a more somber past, notably the tragic collapse during the great flood of 1763 that claimed sixteen lives."}]}]}

title: Picture perfect Irish village
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A quintessential Irish village on the banks of the River Nore, known for its scenic beauty and for being a film location. It offers walking trails, including the Nore Valley Walk, and is adjacent to the Woodstock Gardens & Arboretum."}]}]}

title: Elegant Country Estate Retreat
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" 4-star country estate hotel"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":" "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A grand historic estate set on expansive parkland near Kilkenny, combining classic Georgian architecture with refined luxury, exceptional dining, extensive leisure facilities, and a tranquil yet convenient setting for exploring the medieval city and surrounding countryside."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lyrath Estate is a distinguished five-star property that encapsulates the charm and grandeur of an Irish country estate with all the comforts of a contemporary luxury hotel. Set on over 170 acres of mature gardens and woodlands near Kilkenny, the hotel offers a peaceful retreat while being only a short drive from the city’s historic centre, renowned for its castle, crafts, and vibrant cultural life. Its elegant setting, attentive service, and blend of period character with modern design make it a standout destination in the southeast of Ireland."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes a range of beautifully appointed rooms and suites featuring classic décor, plush bedding, and spacious bathrooms with high-end amenities. Many rooms offer views over the estate’s landscaped gardens and tree-lined avenues. Suites and premium rooms provide extra space and elevated touches, perfect for romantic breaks or special occasions."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dining at Lyrath Estate is a highlight of any stay. The award-winning Park Restaurant serves refined Irish and European cuisine using locally sourced ingredients, while more casual options are available in the Courtyard and other relaxed dining spaces. Afternoon tea and bespoke private dining experiences are also offered, catering to a variety of tastes and occasions."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lyrath Estate’s extensive facilities include a large leisure centre with a swimming pool, sauna, steam room, and fully equipped gym, as well as outdoor walking trails through the estate grounds. The hotel also features elegant event and meeting spaces, making it a popular choice for weddings, conferences, and celebrations."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its combination of historic elegance, modern luxury, and beautiful surroundings, Lyrath Estate offers a memorable and refined experience. It is ideal for couples, families, and travellers seeking both relaxation and exploration in the heart of Kilkenny’s scenic countryside."}]}]}

title: Kilkenny's Medieval Heart
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"In the heart of Kilkenny’s historic centre, St Marys Medieval Mile Museum commands attention as a remarkable blend of architecture, archaeology, and storytelling. Housed within a beautifully restored thirteenth century church, the museum rises above the narrow streets of the Medieval Mile, its stone tower and arched windows reflecting centuries of local history. The setting alone gives visitors an immediate sense of stepping into the city’s layered past."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many traditional museums, St Marys Medieval Mile Museum combines preserved ruins with modern design. Glass walkways and contemporary displays sit alongside ancient tomb slabs, carved stonework, and the remains of earlier structures uncovered during excavation. The contrast between medieval craftsmanship and present day presentation makes the experience especially striking."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about discovery as it is about observation. Moving through the nave and galleries, visitors encounter stories of merchants, bishops, and ordinary townspeople whose lives shaped Kilkenny over generations. Climbing the tower rewards the effort with wide views across the city rooftops, adding a fresh perspective to the historical journey."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the experience. Guided tours bring added depth and detail, while quieter periods allow more time to explore the exhibits at a relaxed pace. Special exhibitions and cultural events throughout the year offer new insights into the city’s heritage."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Access to upper levels includes narrow stairways which may not suit all visitors. The museum is centrally located along the Medieval Mile, with nearby parking and other historic attractions within easy walking distance."}]}]}

title: Historic Underground Cave Tour
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just a short drive from Kilkenny, you'll find a fascinating piece of our history tucked away underground at Dunmore Cave. It's a place where geology and a dark Viking past collide, with some of the most incredible calcite formations you'll see in Ireland. The only way to see the cave is by a guided tour, which really brings the stories of the Viking massacre in 928 AD to life. Be prepared for a bit of a walk, as there are about 700 steps down into the chambers and back up, but it's well worth the effort for the sights within."}]}]}

title: Stylish Stay in Kilkenny
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away on Patrick Street, you'll find Zuni, a stylish boutique hotel that perfectly captures Kilkenny's modern spirit. It's a chic and comfortable home base for exploring the city, with everything just a short stroll away. The real highlight, though, is the award-winning restaurant downstairs, which serves up incredible modern Irish dishes using the best local ingredients."}]}]}

title: Contemporary Irish Cuisine & Bar
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nestled in the heart of the city, just a stone's throw from Kilkenny Castle, you'll find this stylish and cosy spot perfect for a meal or a few drinks. It has a wonderfully relaxed but buzzing atmosphere, whether you're settling into the restaurant or the more casual bar area. They pride themselves on fantastic, award-winning Irish food that champions local producers, offering everything from casual bites to more elaborate farm-to-fork dishes."}]}]}

title: Artistry in Molten Glass
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step into the heart of Irish craftsmanship at Jerpoint Glass Studio, where you can watch master glassblowers transform molten glass into stunning works of art right before your eyes. This family-run studio has a wonderfully welcoming atmosphere, allowing you to get close to the mesmerising action from a viewing area. After seeing the magic happen, browse the beautiful collection in their gallery shop, where you might find the perfect, unique piece to take home as a memory of Kilkenny."}]}]}

title: Artisanal Pastries & Coffee
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For some of the best coffee and most incredible pastries you'll ever taste, you have to pop into this local gem. Everything is baked fresh, so I recommend getting here early before the locals' favourites sell out. The atmosphere is always buzzing and friendly, making it the perfect spot to treat yourself and soak in the town's welcoming vibe."}]}]}

title: A Taste of Local Cavan
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For a real taste of Cavan, you have to pop into Chapter. It's a bright, welcoming spot where you can enjoy delicious, modern Irish food, from a hearty breakfast to lovely cakes and coffee. The place has a great buzz, and what makes it special is the beautiful shop inside, filled with unique gifts and homewares, many from local artists."}]}]}

title: The Storyteller Giant of the Sperrins
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set within the sweeping landscape of the Sperrin Mountains, Nowanois the Storyteller commands attention as one of three remarkable giant sculptures hidden among the hills. Crafted from natural materials by artist Thomas Dambo, this towering wooden figure blends into the surrounding countryside while still sparking immediate wonder. Positioned thoughtfully within the landscape, the sculpture feels both ancient and imaginative, as though it has always belonged there."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many outdoor artworks, Nowanois combines creativity with local identity. Inspired by the geology, folklore, and cultural heritage of the Sperrins, the sculpture reflects a deep connection to place. The contrast between the giant’s playful character and the vast, rugged scenery around him makes the encounter especially memorable."}]}]}

title: Mystical Bronze Age Circles
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Step back into the Bronze Age at this truly magical spot in the Sperrin Mountains. You'll find a mysterious complex of seven stone circles, ancient cairns, and stone rows scattered across the heather-clad moorland, a place that feels alive with history. It's a very short, easy walk from the roadside car park to the stones, allowing you to spend your time wandering among them and soaking up the incredibly tranquil atmosphere of this ancient ceremonial site."}]}]}

title: Hike to a Mountain Giant
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get ready for a rewarding journey to meet Darach, the Guardian Giant, perched atop Mullaghcarn Mountain. Your adventure starts from the Gortin Glen Forest Park car park, following the purple trail on a steady, uphill hike that takes about two hours to reach the summit. The path, a mix of gravel and tarmac, winds through the beautiful forest, opening up to incredible panoramic views long before you even reach the giant sculpture. It's a fantastic walk that rewards your effort with one of the most unique sights and stunning vistas in the Sperrins."}]}]}

title: Stargazer Giant of Davagh
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Deep within the enchanting Davagh Forest, an official Dark Sky Park, you'll discover the magnificent Ceoldán, the Stargazer Giant. This incredible sculpture, crafted from recycled wood, is just a short, gentle walk from the car park, making it an easy adventure for everyone. As you wander along the riverside path, you'll feel the magic of the Sperrins before meeting the giant, kneeling as if to capture the constellations in his hands. It's a truly special experience, connecting art, nature, and the cosmos in one unforgettable visit."}]}]}

title: An Emigrant's Journey Through Time
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"On the outskirts of Omagh, the Ulster American Folk Park commands attention as one of the most immersive heritage attractions in Northern Ireland. Set across a wide open site, the park recreates the journey taken by thousands of emigrants who left Ulster for a new life in America. Historic buildings, costumed guides, and carefully crafted streetscapes give the place a lively and convincing sense of stepping into another time."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many museums, the Ulster American Folk Park combines history with experience. Visitors can walk through traditional Irish cottages, board a full scale emigrant ship, and arrive in a recreated American frontier town, reflecting the challenges and hopes of those who crossed the Atlantic. The contrast between old rural Ireland and the promise of a new world makes the visit especially memorable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about atmosphere as it is about sightseeing. Wandering from building to building, watching craft demonstrations, and hearing stories from guides allows visitors to explore at their own pace. Families, school groups, and history lovers often linger, drawn by the sense of discovery and interaction rather than any formal tour."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the experience. Summer months bring outdoor events and demonstrations that add extra life to the park, while quieter weekdays offer a more relaxed chance to explore. Seasonal activities and themed weekends throughout the year provide new reasons to return."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: The site covers a large area with outdoor paths, so comfortable footwear and weather suitable clothing are recommended. Parking and visitor facilities are available on site, with cafés and picnic areas for longer stays."}]}]}

title: Farm-Fresh Premium Ice Cream on the farm
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked away on a family dairy farm in the Tyrone countryside, you'll find some of the creamiest, most luxurious ice cream imaginable. Dukes is a real hidden gem where the owners' passion shines through in every scoop, using milk from their own cows and the best local ingredients to create incredible flavours. It’s a charming, self-serve shop right in the farmyard, so don't be surprised if you're greeted by a friendly farm dog or a few curious chickens on your way to the freezers."}]}]}

title: Forest Park Adventure
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tucked into the rolling Sperrin Mountains, Gortin Glen Forest Park commands attention as one of the most scenic outdoor escapes in County Tyrone. Surrounded by dense woodland, open moorland, and quiet country roads, the park offers wide views across hills and valleys that give the area a peaceful and natural presence. From almost every path, the landscape feels spacious, wild, and beautifully unspoiled."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many forest parks, Gortin Glen combines scenery with variety. A network of driving routes, walking trails, and cycle paths allows visitors to explore the changing terrain at their own pace. The contrast between sheltered tree lined sections and high open viewpoints makes the experience especially memorable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about relaxation as it is about activity. Families picnic beside streams, walkers follow gentle trails, and photographers pause to capture the colours and wildlife of the Sperrins. Visitors often linger here, drawn by the fresh air and sense of calm rather than any formal attraction."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the experience. Summer brings long bright days and lively greenery, while autumn fills the forest with rich reds and golds. Early mornings and quiet evenings tend to be especially peaceful, with deer and birds more easily spotted along the paths."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Weather in the hills can change quickly, so warm clothing and sturdy footwear are recommended. Parking and visitor facilities are available within the park, with clearly marked routes suitable for a range of abilities."}]}]}

title: Thrills in the Countryside
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Todds Leap commands attention as one of Northern Ireland’s most exciting outdoor adventure destinations. Surrounded by open fields, woodland, and rugged terrain, the centre offers a lively and energetic setting where the sounds of engines, laughter, and activity fill the air. The wide rural landscape gives the place a sense of freedom and space that adds to the thrill of every visit."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many attractions, Todds Leap combines adventure with variety. More than twenty different activities are available, from off road driving and clay pigeon shooting to a towering zip line and a giant swing. The contrast between high adrenaline challenges and gentler family friendly options makes the experience especially appealing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about fun as it is about excitement. Groups of friends test their nerve on daring courses, families explore woodland trails, and younger visitors enjoy the on site zoo and play areas. Guests often linger here, drawn by the buzz of activity and the chance to try something new rather than any formal attraction."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the experience. Bright summer days bring the centre to life with outdoor events and busy activity sessions, while quieter weekdays offer a more relaxed chance to enjoy the facilities. Special group packages and seasonal events throughout the year add extra energy and atmosphere."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Many activities require advance booking and have age or height restrictions, so planning ahead is recommended. Parking and visitor facilities are available on site, with safety equipment and professional instruction provided for all adventure experiences."}]}]}

title: Artisanal Coffee and Brunch
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Right in the heart of Ballymena, you'll find what many locals consider the best coffee and brunch spot around. It’s a welcoming place with a modern vibe, perfect for catching up with friends or just enjoying a quiet moment. The food is always exceptional, with creative dishes and beautifully presented plates that taste as good as they look, and the staff are known for being incredibly friendly."}]}]}

title: A Cozy Antrim Gem
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"My personal Favourite! Tucked away on Church Street, you'll find a real local gem where the passion for quality coffee and food shines through. It's more than just a coffee shop; it's a community hub with a wonderfully cozy atmosphere perfect for a relaxing brunch. Don't leave without trying the Coconut cappuccino , it's a firm favourite of mine for a reason and perfectly showcases their commitment to using fresh, local ingredients including their own roasted coffee"}]}]}

title: Lakeside Forest Hideaway
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" Boutique luxury woodland retreat"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","text":" "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A serene cabin hideaway set deep within Killykeen Forest Park on the shores of Lough Oughter, offering design-led timber cabins, nature-based experiences, and a peaceful escape into Ireland’s lake district with plenty of outdoor activities and wellness options."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Cabu by the Lakes is a distinctive nature-focused holiday destination nestled in the heart of County Cavan’s Killykeen Forest Park. Surrounded by lush woodland and set directly beside the scenic lakes of Lough Oughter, this unique property blends stylish cabin living with the tranquillity of the natural environment, making it a perfect escape from everyday life."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation features a collection of beautifully crafted log cabins and lake houses scattered throughout the forest. These cabins range from intimate one-bedroom retreats to spacious multi-bedroom boathouses, all designed with open-plan living, cosy wood-burning stoves, outdoor dining areas, and thoughtful interiors that create a warm and relaxing atmosphere. Each unit is fully equipped for self-catering, providing both comfort and independence for guests."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"At the heart of the resort is the Cabin Club, a welcoming social space offering casual dining, drinks, and a relaxed communal environment. Guests can also make use of the on-site shop for local produce, treats, and essentials, adding to the effortless, laid-back feel of the stay."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The setting encourages outdoor living and adventure. Visitors can explore forest trails on foot or by bike, enjoy kayaking and paddleboarding on the lake, try their hand at fishing, or simply unwind surrounded by nature. Wellness facilities such as hot tubs and saunas add an extra element of indulgence, making Cabu an ideal destination for relaxation as well as activity."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"With its combination of stylish accommodation, breathtaking natural surroundings, and a focus on wellbeing and outdoor experiences, Cabu by the Lakes is perfect for couples, families, and small groups seeking a tranquil and rejuvenating getaway in one of Ireland’s most beautiful rural landscapes."}]}]}

title: Lakeside Forest Escape
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"On the shores of Lough Oughter in County Cavan, Killykeen Forest Park commands attention as one of the most peaceful and scenic woodland escapes in Ireland. Surrounded by lakes, islands, and tall stands of pine and broadleaf trees, the park offers gentle views that feel calm and unspoiled. Quiet pathways and rippling water give the area a relaxed and welcoming presence throughout the year."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many outdoor locations, Killykeen Forest Park combines nature with recreation. Walking trails, cycling routes, and fishing spots reflect a place where visitors can enjoy both activity and tranquillity. The contrast between dense forest paths and open lakeside scenery makes the experience especially refreshing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about atmosphere as it is about exploration. Strolling beneath the trees, watching boats drift across the water, or simply sitting by the shoreline allows visitors to enjoy the park at their own pace. Families, anglers, and nature lovers often linger here, drawn by the sense of space and quiet rather than any formal attraction."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the experience. Bright summer days bring warm light to the lakes and busy picnic areas, while autumn adds rich colour to the woodland. Early mornings and late evenings tend to be especially peaceful, with wildlife more easily spotted along the trails."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Paths around the park are generally accessible, though some areas may be uneven or muddy after rain. Parking and basic visitor facilities are available on site, with short walks leading directly to the main lakeside viewpoints."}]}]}

title: Historic Battlefield & Gardens
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Set within the rolling parklands of Oldbridge Estate, the Battle of the Boyne Visitor Centre commands attention as one of Ireland’s most important historical sites. Overlooking the River Boyne, the elegant eighteenth century house and surrounding grounds provide a peaceful setting that belies the dramatic events that once unfolded here. Wide views across the fields help visitors picture the famous battle that shaped the course of Irish and European history."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many historic locations, the centre combines landscape with story. Interactive exhibits and detailed displays explain the events of 1690 and the clash between the armies of King William and King James. The contrast between the calm riverside scenery and the turbulent past it represents makes the experience especially engaging."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about understanding as it is about sightseeing. Exploring the exhibition rooms, watching audiovisual presentations, and walking the marked trails through the battlefield allows visitors to connect with the history at their own pace. Families, school groups, and history enthusiasts often linger, drawn by the clear storytelling and open surroundings rather than any formal attraction."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the visit. Sunny days are ideal for following the outdoor trails and enjoying views along the river, while quieter mornings offer a more relaxed chance to explore the centre itself. Seasonal events and guided tours throughout the year add extra depth to the experience."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: The estate includes accessible paths, a café, and picnic areas. Parking is available on site, with easy routes leading from the visitor centre to the main points of interest across the historic battlefield."}]}]}

title: Historic Kells Guesthouse
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Star rating:"},{"type":"text","text":" Boutique luxury bed & breakfast"},{"type":"hardBreak"},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"What makes it unique:"},{"type":"text","text":" A beautifully restored 19th-century heritage house in the heart of Kells, County Meath, offering individually furnished en-suite rooms, personalised hospitality, and a warm, boutique experience ideal for exploring Ireland’s Ancient East."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tom Blake House is a charming, family-run guesthouse set in a historic property originally built in 1890 and thoughtfully reimagined as luxury accommodation. The owners combine the cosy warmth of a traditional Irish B&B with the comfort and style of a boutique hotel, creating a welcoming environment for couples, families, and small groups. The house features light-filled reception rooms, curated cosy corners with open fires, and elegant interiors designed for relaxation and indulgence."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Accommodation includes five individually designed en-suite bedrooms with luxurious beds and contemporary comforts, each crafted to provide a peaceful and memorable stay. Guests enjoy a highly regarded breakfast made from seasonal, locally sourced ingredients. Whether planning a tranquil escape, a cultural trip through the Boyne Valley, or a special gathering with friends or family, the boutique atmosphere makes every stay feel personal and unique."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Located in the historic market town of Kells, Tom Blake House is ideally placed for exploring nearby heritage attractions such as monastic sites, ancient ruins, and scenic landscapes. Its combination of historic charm, contemporary comfort, and attentive service makes it a standout choice for travellers seeking an authentic Irish stay infused with character and hospitality."}]}]}

title: Ireland's Theme Park & Zoo Adventure
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get ready for a day of thrills and animal encounters at Emerald Park, a fantastic spot where rollercoasters and a zoo combine for an action-packed adventure. You can brave the legendary Cú Chulainn Coaster, one of Europe's largest wooden rollercoasters, or take a walk on the wild side through the zoo, home to a wonderful range of animals. It's a sprawling park, so expect a fair bit of walking, but everything is easily accessible right from the entrance."}]}]}

title: Journey into Ancient Ireland
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Newgrange commands attention as one of the most extraordinary ancient monuments in the world. This vast stone passage tomb, older than the pyramids of Egypt, sits at the centre of a peaceful bend in the River Boyne, with sweeping views over the surrounding countryside. Its great circular mound and carefully carved entrance stones give the site a powerful and timeless presence."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many prehistoric sites, Newgrange combines engineering with mystery. Built over five thousand years ago, it reflects a deep understanding of astronomy, architecture, and ritual. The contrast between the simple grassy exterior and the finely constructed inner passage makes the experience especially memorable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about atmosphere as it is about sightseeing. Stepping inside the narrow passage and entering the central chamber allows visitors to imagine the ceremonies and beliefs of the people who created it. Families, historians, and curious travellers often linger, drawn by the sense of wonder and ancient heritage rather than any ordinary attraction."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the visit. Bright days highlight the scale of the monument and the beauty of the valley, while misty weather adds an air of mystery to the site. Special winter solstice tours reveal how the rising sun once illuminated the inner chamber, bringing the monument to life in a remarkable way."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Access to Newgrange is by guided tour only and tickets must be obtained from the visitor centre at Brú na Bóinne. Parking and facilities are available there, with shuttle buses transporting visitors to the monument itself."}]}]}

title: Ireland's Largest Norman Fortress
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Rising above the River Boyne, Trim Castle commands attention as the largest Anglo Norman castle in Ireland. Dominating the skyline of the town of Trim, its massive stone walls, central keep, and surrounding moat create an imposing sight that has stood for more than eight centuries. From every angle the fortress feels powerful and enduring, a true symbol of medieval strength."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Unlike many historic ruins, Trim Castle combines architecture with story. Built in the twelfth century as a stronghold of Norman power, it reflects a time when castles were both military defenses and centres of authority. The contrast between the solid stone exterior and the surprisingly complex interior design makes the setting especially memorable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Visiting is as much about atmosphere as it is about sightseeing. Walking through the grounds, crossing the stone bridges, and climbing into the great keep allows visitors to imagine life in a turbulent period of Irish history. Families, history enthusiasts, and film fans often linger, drawn by the sense of stepping directly into the past rather than any ordinary attraction."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Timing can transform the visit. Bright days reveal long views across the surrounding countryside, while grey skies and changing weather add drama to the ancient walls. Early mornings and quieter afternoons tend to be more peaceful, allowing the castle and riverside setting to feel even more atmospheric."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Note: Access to the keep is by guided tour and involves steep steps, so comfortable footwear is recommended. Parking and visitor facilities are available nearby, with easy walks connecting the castle to the town centre."}]}]}

title: Ancient Royal Seat of Ireland
description: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Come and stand where Ireland's High Kings once ruled on the ancient and sacred Hill of Tara.
